__data__.text: Narada describes Rama to Valmiki as a virtuous and powerful man. Rama is said to be truthful, resolute, and compassionate towards all beings. He is intelligent, eloquent, and a skilled warrior with an impressive physique.  He is also described as wise, pure, self-controlled, and knowledgeable in scriptures and archery.  Rama upholds dharma and protects all living beings.

Rama is described as virtuous, wise, and skilled. He is compared to deities like Brahma and Vishnu, possessing qualities like forbearance and prowess. He is loved by all and upholds dharma. King Dasaratha wished to appoint Rama as regent, but Queen Kaikeyi invoked a previous boon, requesting Rama's exile and her son Bharata's coronation. Bound by his word, Dasaratha sorrowfully exiled Rama, who obeyed his father's command to honor the boon granted to Kaikeyi.

Rama, obedient to his father and honoring Kaikeyi's boon, went into exile.  He was followed by his devoted brother Lakshmana, who was known for his modesty and affection for Rama.  Sita, Rama's beloved wife and a noblewoman, also accompanied him.  The citizens and King Dasaratha himself followed the departing trio for some distance.

Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita met Guha, the Nishad chief, and sent their charioteer back to Ayodhya.  They journeyed through forests and across rivers, eventually reaching Chitrakut on the advice of Sage Bharadwaja. There, they built a dwelling and lived happily.  Meanwhile, heartbroken by Rama's exile, King Dasaratha passed away.  Bharata, though offered the throne, refused it and went to Chitrakut to implore Rama to return and rule.

Bharata urged Rama to take the throne, but Rama, honoring his father's promise, declined.  He gave Bharata his sandals as symbols of authority and urged him to rule in his stead.  Bharata, disappointed but respectful, returned to Ayodhya with the sandals.  He ruled from Nandigrama, longing for Rama's return.

After Bharata's departure, Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita entered the Dandaka forest.  They encountered several sages, including Agastya, who gifted Rama divine weapons.  The sages requested Rama's protection from the demons plaguing the forest.  Rama vowed to destroy these demons.  In the same forest, they encountered the demoness Surpanakha, who was subsequently disfigured.  After defeating and cremating a demon, who was restored to his Gandharva form, Rama was advised to seek out the wise hermitess Sabari.  Honored by Sabari, Rama then met Hanuman, the monkey chief, by the Pampa lake.  Hanuman, on Mount Rsyamuka, described Vali's great strength to Rama, as Sugriva was apprehensive about Rama's prowess against him.

After Bharata's return to Ayodhya, Rama, refusing the kingship out of respect for his father's promise, gave Bharata his sandals as symbols of his authority.  Bharata, though disappointed, accepted this and ruled from Nandigrama, yearning for Rama's return.  Meanwhile, Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita journeyed deeper into the Dandaka forest.  There they encountered various sages, receiving divine weapons from Agastya and promising to protect the sages from the demons troubling them.  Rama then encountered and disfigured the demoness Surpanakha.  This led to a battle with other demons, including Khara, Trisira, and Dusana, whom Rama defeated.  Hearing of this, Ravana, enraged by the death of his kinsmen, plotted revenge. He consulted Marica, who advised against confronting Rama, but Ravana, driven by his anger, ignored this counsel.  With Marica's help, Ravana abducted Sita after fatally wounding Jatayu, who tried to rescue her.  Grieving Sita's abduction and Jatayu's demise, Rama cremated the noble bird.  While searching for Sita, Rama and Lakshmana encountered the monstrous Kabandha.  Rama displayed his immense strength by effortlessly throwing Kabandha's skeletal remains a great distance.  To further demonstrate his power and assure Sugriva, Rama effortlessly pierced seven palmyra trees, a hillock, and even reached the subterranean realm with a single arrow.  Thus reassured, Sugriva led Rama to Kiskindha.  A confrontation with Vali ensued, and Rama swiftly killed Vali with an arrow, subsequently installing Sugriva on the throne.  Sugriva then mobilized his monkey army to search for Sita.  Hanuman located Sita in Lanka, imprisoned in an Asoka grove.  He presented Rama's signet ring to Sita, reassured her of Rama's imminent arrival, and then proceeded to create havoc, destroying parts of the city and defeating several of Ravana's warriors before finally being captured.

Rama, residing in the Dandaka forest, vanquished Khara, Trisira, Dusana, and fourteen thousand of their demon followers after Surpanakha instigated them to attack.  Ravana, upon learning of his kinsmen's deaths, became enraged and sought Marica's assistance for revenge. Despite Marica's warnings against confronting Rama, Ravana, consumed by anger, disregarded the advice. With Marica's deceptive aid, Ravana abducted Sita after mortally wounding Jatayu, who valiantly attempted to protect her.  Overwhelmed with grief, Rama cremated Jatayu and continued searching for Sita, eventually encountering the monstrous Kabandha. After defeating Kabandha, Rama cremated him, and the demon, restored to his original Gandharva form, ascended to heaven, advising Rama to seek the wise hermitess Sabari. Following this counsel, Rama met Sabari and subsequently encountered Hanuman. Through Hanuman's assistance, Rama befriended Sugriva and recounted his story, including Sita's abduction. Sugriva, moved by Rama's plight, forged an alliance, and Rama vowed to slay Vali.  Hanuman, having located Sita in Lanka, returned to inform Rama.  Rama then traveled to the southern ocean with Sugriva and, through a display of power, prompted the ocean god to appear and apologize for obstructing their passage. With the ocean god's cooperation, Rama had Nala construct a bridge to Lanka.  After slaying Ravana in battle and rescuing Sita, Rama expressed harsh words towards her, causing her to enter fire to prove her purity. Rama then installed Vibhisana as Lanka's ruler and, feeling a sense of accomplishment, rejoiced.  After receiving a boon from the gods to revive his fallen allies, Rama returned to Ayodhya in the Pushpaka chariot, accompanied by Sita, Lakshmana, Vibhisana, Sugriva, and others.  Stopping at the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja, Rama sent Hanuman ahead to inform Bharata of his return.

Rama, with the help of Hanuman, formed a friendship with Sugriva.  Rama shared his life story, including Sita's abduction, with Sugriva.  Sugriva, in turn, recounted his conflict with his brother Vali.  To demonstrate Vali's strength, Sugriva showed Rama the large skeleton of Dundubhi, whom Vali had killed.  Rama, in a display of his own power, effortlessly tossed the skeleton a great distance and pierced seven trees, a hill, and even the subterranean levels with a single arrow.  This reassured Sugriva, and together they went to Kiskindha.  Sugriva's roar drew Vali out, and Rama killed Vali with an arrow. Rama then installed Sugriva as the new ruler. Sugriva sent monkeys to search for Sita.  Hanuman, following Sampati's advice, leaped across the ocean to Lanka.  After Rama rescued Sita and regained his kingdom, he ruled for many years, bringing peace and prosperity.  The verses conclude by extolling the virtues of reading the Ramayana, promising blessings and absolution from sins.

Hanuman, advised by Sampati, leaped across the vast ocean to Lanka. There, he found Sita in an Asoka grove.  He gave her Rama's ring and reassured her of Rama's imminent arrival. Hanuman then proceeded to create a disturbance, defeating several of Ravana's warriors before being captured. He allowed himself to be taken to Ravana, but then set fire to much of Lanka before returning to Rama with news of Sita.  After hearing Hanuman's report, Rama went to the southern coast and displayed his power to the ocean, eventually convincing the ocean god to allow for the construction of a bridge. Rama crossed the bridge, defeated Ravana, and retrieved Sita. However, he publicly questioned Sita's purity, leading her to enter a fire. The fire-god testified to Sita's innocence, and Rama accepted her back. Rama's victory and Sita's vindication brought joy to all.

Rama, vindicated by the fire-god's testimony of Sita's purity, joyfully accepted her back.  The gods, sages, and all beings rejoiced at this triumph over Ravana and Sita's rescue.  Rama then crowned Vibhishana king of Lanka, fulfilling his purpose.  After reviving his fallen allies with a boon from the gods, Rama, along with Sita, Lakshmana, Vibhishana, Sugriva, and others, departed for Ayodhya in the Pushpaka chariot.  Stopping at Sage Bharadwaja's hermitage, Rama sent Hanuman ahead to inform Bharata of his return.  He then continued to Nandigrama, recounting the events leading to his exile.  At Nandigrama, Rama and his brothers disentangled their matted hair, symbolizing the end of their exile, and reclaimed their kingdom.  Rama's reign ushered in a golden age of peace, prosperity, and piety.  His subjects were joyful, healthy, and free from fear.  There was no famine, disease, or crime. Families remained whole, and everyone lived in harmony.  Rama performed numerous sacrifices and generously distributed wealth, ensuring the flourishing of his kingdom and upholding the social order.

Rama's reign was a golden age, marked by happiness reminiscent of the Satyayuga. He performed numerous horse sacrifices and other rituals involving vast quantities of gold.  He generously donated countless cows to scholars and immense riches to Brahmins.  Rama also strengthened royal dynasties, supporting them both politically and financially, leading to unprecedented prosperity.  He guided people of all social classes to adhere to their respective duties. After ruling for eleven thousand years, Rama ascended to Brahmaloka.  Those who read the sacred story of Rama, considered as holy as the Vedas, are cleansed of all sins.  Reading this life-affirming narrative ensures heavenly honor, accompanied by one's descendants and followers.  The benefits of reading the Ramayana extend to all classes: Brahmins gain eloquence, Kshatriyas attain kingship, merchants prosper in trade, and even Sudras achieve a higher status.

Brahma’s visit

Canto II

Brahma’s visit

__data__.text: A sage, Valmiki, honored the wise Narada after hearing his discourse.  Narada, after receiving due respect, departed for the heavens. Valmiki then went to the nearby Tamasa river for his ablutions.  He pointed out a pleasant, clear portion of the riverbank to his disciple, Bharadwaja, instructing him to prepare for his bath.  Bharadwaja dutifully assisted the sage. While walking along the riverbank observing the forest, Valmiki witnessed a hunter shoot a male crane from a pair.  The female crane, seeing her mate fall, cried out in grief. Moved by compassion for the crane and angered by the hunter's cruel act, Valmiki cursed him, saying that he would never find peace for killing the creature during a moment of intimacy.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto II

Brahma’s visit

__data__.text: Witnessing the hunter's cruel act, Valmiki felt compassion for the grieving female crane.  Angered by the hunter's disregard for the bird's life, particularly during a moment of intimacy, Valmiki cursed him, proclaiming that the hunter would never know peace.  Reflecting on his words, Valmiki realized the unintentional rhythmic and structured nature of his curse. He declared these words to be poetry, specifically defining its metrical form to his disciple Bharadwaja. After completing his ablutions and returning to his hermitage, Valmiki continued to contemplate this incident and the verse he had uttered.  Brahma, the four-faced creator, then arrived at the hermitage.  Astonished, Valmiki rose quickly to greet the deity, offering him traditional respects and a seat.  Brahma accepted the offered seat and then gestured for Valmiki to sit as well.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto II

Brahma’s visit

__data__.text: Brahma, the creator, visited Valmiki's hermitage.  Astonished, Valmiki respectfully greeted and offered Brahma a seat.  After Brahma was seated, he instructed Valmiki to sit as well.  Despite Brahma's presence, Valmiki remained lost in thought, reflecting on the hunter's act and the verse he had uttered. He lamented the hunter's needless killing of the crane.  Brahma reassured Valmiki, stating the verse was divinely inspired. He then commissioned Valmiki to compose an epic poem narrating the life of Lord Rama, assuring him of its accuracy and enduring popularity.  After imparting this task, Brahma vanished.  Valmiki and his disciples were filled with wonder, repeating the verse and marveling at the honor bestowed upon their teacher.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto II

Brahma’s visit

__data__.text: Brahma declared that Valmiki's verse about the slain crane was divinely inspired and destined for enduring fame.  He prophesied that the story of Rama, composed by Valmiki, would remain popular as long as mountains and rivers exist on Earth.  Brahma then vanished, leaving Valmiki and his disciples in awe.  The disciples, amazed by the honor bestowed upon their teacher, repeated the verse. They realized that Valmiki’s grief had spontaneously transformed into poetic verse. This inspired Valmiki to compose the entire Ramayana in the same meter, creating a beautiful and renowned epic poem narrating the life of Rama.  The narrator invites the audience to listen to this epic tale, crafted with eloquent language and harmonious structure, recounting the noble deeds of Rama and culminating in the defeat of Ravana.

Canto III
A brief outline of the Ramayana as given by the poet himself

__data__.text: Valmiki, inspired by Narada's account of Rama's life, sought to gain deeper insight into the prince's story through meditation. He visualized Rama, Lakshmana, Sita, King Dasharatha, and their interactions.  Valmiki also foresaw the events of Rama's exile with Sita and Lakshmana, witnessing the past and future as clearly as an object held in his hand. He then decided to compose the epic poem, recounting the story as narrated by Narada. This epic, focusing on Rama's life, touches upon themes of worldly desires and spiritual pursuits. It highlights Rama's birth, strength, kindness, popularity, patience, gentleness, and honesty. The poem includes the story of the Ganga's descent, Rama's journey with Viswamitra, the breaking of the bow, and Sita's wedding.  It also covers the conflict between Rama and Parashurama, Dasharatha's virtues, Rama's planned coronation, Kaikeyi's betrayal, the exile, Dasharatha's sorrow and death, the people's grief, and Rama's encounter with the Nishadha chief. Finally, the verses depict Rama's crossing of the Ganga, his visit to Sage Bharadwaja, and his subsequent journey to Chitrakuta.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto III
A brief outline of the Ramayana as given by the poet himself

__data__.text: Valmiki's narration continues, highlighting key events in Rama's life.  The poem describes the disagreement between Rama and Parashurama after Rama broke Shiva's bow. It then recounts King Dasharatha's virtues and the preparations for Rama's coronation, which was disrupted by Kaikeyi's manipulative actions. This led to Rama's exile, causing immense grief to Dasharatha and ultimately leading to his death. The citizens of Ayodhya mourned deeply, and those who followed Rama into exile were persuaded to return. The poem then narrates Rama's conversation with the Nishadha chief and his dismissal of the charioteer. It describes Rama crossing the Ganga river, visiting Sage Bharadwaja, and then proceeding to Chitrakuta on the sage's advice.  At Chitrakuta, Rama built a hut and settled with Sita and Lakshmana. Bharata arrived and tried to convince Rama to return to Ayodhya and claim the throne. Rama performed the last rites for his father.  Bharata then placed Rama's sandals on the throne as a symbol of Rama's rightful rule and resided in Nandigrama. Fearing further disturbance from the people of Ayodhya, Rama moved to the Dandaka forest and killed the demon Viradha.  He then visited Sage Sarabhanga and met Sutiksna, a disciple of Sage Agastya. Sita met Anasuya, the wife of Sage Atri, who gifted her tokens of goodwill.  Rama visited and was honored by Sage Agastya, who presented him with a divine bow.  The narrative then turns to Surpanakha's proposal to Rama and her subsequent disfigurement by Lakshmana.  The poem continues with the slaying of the demons Khara, Trisira, and Dusana, followed by Ravana's plot to kidnap Sita.  Rama killed Marica, who was disguised as a golden deer, which led to Ravana's abduction of Sita.  Rama mourned Sita's disappearance and lamented the death of Jatayu, the king of the vultures, who tried to rescue her. He encountered Kabandha and reached Lake Pampa.  He visited Shabri and ate the fruits she offered.  He grieved for Sita and met Hanuman at Lake Pampa. He then arrived at Mount Rsyamuka, where he met Sugriva, who was hiding from his brother Vali. Rama befriended Sugriva and aided him in defeating Vali.  After Vali's death, Sugriva was installed as king. Tara, Vali's widow, mourned her husband's death.  Rama and Sugriva agreed to search for Sita after the monsoon season. During the monsoon, Rama stayed on Mount Rsyamuka.  Sugriva gathered his forces to search for Sita and sent them in all directions after Rama expressed his impatience.  The monkeys searching south, led by Angada, found a bear's cave and, disheartened, began fasting near the seashore. There, they encountered Sampati.  Hanuman climbed Mount Mahendra to leap across the ocean. He spoke with Mount Mainaka, which rose from the sea at the sea-god's behest.

Canto III
A brief outline of the Ramayana as given by the poet himself

__data__.text: After Sugriva's coronation following Vali's death, his wife Tara mourned her husband. Rama and Sugriva agreed to search for Sita after the monsoon season, during which Rama resided on Mount Rsyamuka.  Impatient, Rama prompted Sugriva, who then assembled and dispatched his monkey forces in all directions to search for Sita.  The group searching south, led by Angada, found a bear's cave and, discouraged by their lack of progress, began fasting near the seashore. There, they encountered Sampati. Hanuman then climbed Mount Mahendra to leap across the ocean, speaking with Mount Mainaka as it rose from the sea.  Upon reaching Lanka, Hanuman faced threats from Surasa and Lankini, and defeated the demoness Simhika.  He entered Lanka stealthily, observing Ravana and his aerial car, the Pushpaka. He found Sita in the Ashoka grove and gave her Rama's ring. After Sita gave Hanuman a jewel, he destroyed part of the grove to demonstrate his strength and reassure her.  He then set Lanka ablaze and returned to Rama with news of Sita and her jewel. Rama, with Sugriva and the monkey army, then journeyed to Lanka, building a bridge across the ocean with the help of Nala. They besieged Lanka, and with Vibhishana's counsel, Rama defeated Kumbhakarna, Meghanada, and finally Ravana, rescuing Sita. Vibhishana was crowned king of Lanka.  Rama, Sita, and their party returned to Ayodhya in the Pushpaka, stopping at Prayaga to meet Sage Bharadwaja. Hanuman was sent ahead to inform Bharata of their return.  Rama's coronation followed, after which he dismissed his army.

__data__.text: The verses describe the climactic events of the Ramayana and its aftermath.  First, the major battles and Sita's rescue are recounted: Kumbhakarna's and Meghanada's deaths, Ravana's defeat, and Sita's recovery in Lanka.  Following this, Vibhishana's coronation as the new king of Lanka occurs.  Rama then uses Ravana's aerial vehicle, the Pushpaka, to begin his journey back to Ayodhya, stopping at Prayaga to visit Sage Bharadwaja. Hanuman is sent ahead to inform Bharata of Rama's return. The verses conclude by mentioning Rama's coronation, the disbanding of his army, and the poet Valmiki's comprehensive account of Rama's life, including future events.

Canto IV

After his ascension to the throne of Ayodhya, Sri Rama listens to his

__data__.text: Valmiki, a revered sage, composed an epic poem narrating the life of Rama. This poem, rich in expressive language and meaningful themes, recounts Rama's journey, including his return to his kingdom after exile.  The epic comprises twenty-four thousand verses, divided into six sections and an epilogue. After completing the poem, Valmiki sought someone with exceptional memory to recite it.  At that time, Rama's sons, Kusa and Lava, approached the sage. Raised by Valmiki, these young princes were knowledgeable in their duties, musically gifted, and well-versed in the Vedas.  Recognizing their talents, Valmiki taught them the Ramayana to further their understanding of the scriptures. The poem, focusing on Sita's story and the defeat of Ravana, was enjoyable to recite and adaptable to various musical styles.  Kusa and Lava, skilled musicians with melodious voices, mastered the poem and performed it for an audience of sages and other respected individuals. Their captivating performance and resemblance to Rama added to the impact of the epic's retelling.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto IV

After his ascension to the throne of Ayodhya, Sri Rama listens to his
own story as sung by his sons (Kusa and Lava)

__data__.text: Kusa and Lava, having memorized the virtuous epic poem Ramayana, recited it to an assembly of sages and pious individuals. The princes, deeply focused, chanted the verses as instructed by Valmiki.  Impressed by their performance, the sages, with tearful eyes, praised the boys, exclaiming "Well done! Excellent!".  The sages, lovers of virtue, commended Kusa and Lava for their melodious singing and the captivating recitation, noting how vividly the poem brought the past to life.  As the princes continued their enchanting performance, moved by the story, a sage offered them a pitcher. Other sages, equally moved, presented them with various gifts: bark for clothing, a deerskin, a sacred thread, a drinking vessel, a grass girdle, a mat, a cloth, a hatchet, brown-red cloth, a scarf, a string for their hair, a cord for wood, a sacrificial vessel, a wood bundle, and a wooden seat. Some invoked blessings upon them, while others wished them long life. The truthful sages declared the narrative wonderful and predicted it would inspire future poets, praising Kusa and Lava's skillful and beautiful rendition of the song.

Canto IV

After his ascension to the throne of Ayodhya, Sri Rama listens to his
own story as sung by his sons (Kusa and Lava)

__data__.text: Kusa and Lava received further gifts from the assembled sages: items for ascetic life like a string for hair, cord for wood, a sacrificial vessel, and firewood.  Some sages blessed the boys, while others wished them long life.  The truthful sages declared the Ramayana a wonderful narrative, predicting it would be a source of inspiration for future poets and praising Kusa and Lava’s beautiful performance.  They described the epic as conducive to long life and prosperity, pleasing to both ear and mind.  One day, Rama observed the two boys singing in the streets of Ayodhya, receiving praise everywhere.  He brought them to his palace, offered them honorable seats, and entertained them.  Seated on his throne, surrounded by his ministers and brothers, Rama, impressed by the boys' appearance and modesty, requested his brothers Bharata, Lakshmana, and Shatrughna to listen attentively to the narrative. He urged Kusa and Lava to begin, and they sang the well-known story in a melodious, captivating voice reminiscent of a lute.  Their song resonated deeply with the audience, bringing joy and touching their hearts.  Rama noted the boys' princely qualities despite their ascetic appearance and encouraged his brothers to listen to the story, which he believed would bring him well-being.  Prompted by Rama, the boys began reciting the epic in the Marga style, and Rama, joining the assembly, became absorbed in their performance.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto V
A description of the city of Ayodhya

__data__.text: The kingdom of Kosala, a prosperous land rich in grain and wealth, lies along the Sarayu River. Within it sits the renowned city of Ayodhya, built by Manu, the first human.  Twelve yojanas long and three wide, its well-planned roads are sprinkled with water and strewn with flowers.  King Dasaratha ruled it wisely, increasing its prosperity.  Ayodhya boasts arched gateways, bustling markets, weapons, and skilled craftspeople.  The city is filled with musicians, tall buildings adorned with flags, and defensive weaponry.  Troupes of dancers entertain, mango groves provide shade, and a strong wall protects the city. A deep moat makes it difficult for enemies to approach. Horses, elephants, cows, camels, and donkeys abound within the city walls. Feudatory princes regularly arrive to pay tribute, and traders from many lands contribute to its vibrant atmosphere.  Grand mansions and numerous attics give Ayodhya the majestic appearance of Indra's celestial city, Amaravati.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto V
A description of the city of Ayodhya

Canto V
A description of the city of Ayodhya

__data__.text: Ayodhya, a grand city, was adorned with gardens, mango groves, and a strong defensive wall and a deep moat, making it nearly impregnable.  The city was bustling with activity, filled with horses, elephants, cows, camels, donkeys, and visiting feudal princes paying tribute. Traders from various lands contributed to its thriving marketplace.  Towering mansions and numerous attics gave it the grandeur of Amaravati, Indra's celestial city.  Laid out like a game board, Ayodhya was vibrant and colorful, teeming with beautiful women and overflowing with precious stones.  Seven-story buildings graced the cityscape, and the city was well-organized on level ground.  An abundance of rice and sweet water contributed to its prosperity.  The sounds of kettledrums, lutes, and drums resonated throughout the city.  Ayodhya, populated by noble men, resembled a celestial city with its well-arranged houses.  The city was also defended by skilled warriors who adhered to a strict code of honor in combat, refusing to attack isolated, defenseless, or fleeing opponents.  These warriors were adept at archery and other weaponry, capable of defeating even fierce wild animals. King Dasaratha ruled over this magnificent city, which was also home to learned Brahmanas, masters of the Vedas and other sacred knowledge, and righteous, generous seers.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto VI

A description of the city of Ayodhya and the prosperity of its inhabitants
during the reign of King Dasaratha

Canto VI

A description of the city of Ayodhya and the prosperity of its inhabitants
during the reign of King Dasaratha

__data__.text: King Dasaratha ruled Ayodhya, learned in the Vedas, and ensured his subjects' well-being.  He was respected and loved by all, both within the city and the surrounding countryside. As a descendant of Ikswaku, he was a skilled warrior and a devoted follower of dharma, controlling his senses and performing sacrifices. His reputation extended throughout the three worlds.  Dasaratha was strong, with many allies, and comparable to Indra and Kubera in wealth.  Like Manu, he protected his people.  Truthful and dedicated to dharma, artha, and kama, he ruled Ayodhya as Indra ruled Amaravati. His citizens were content, virtuous, and knowledgeable.  Every household was prosperous, possessing ample resources and achieving life's goals.  No one in Ayodhya was found to be greedy, cruel, or ignorant.  The people were joyful and morally upright.  All were adorned and enjoyed life's comforts, using perfumes and maintaining a clean appearance. Everyone ate pure food, gave generously, and practiced self-control.  No one lacked jewelry or possessions.  The citizens worshipped the sacred fire, performed sacrifices, and were free from immoral behavior.  The Brahmins were dedicated to their duties, controlled their senses, studied diligently, and were reluctant to accept gifts. Everyone was knowledgeable, truthful, and generous.  All were well-versed in sacred texts and observed pious vows. There was no suffering or misery in Ayodhya.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto VI

A description of the city of Ayodhya and the prosperity of its inhabitants
during the reign of King Dasaratha

Canto VI

A description of the city of Ayodhya and the prosperity of its inhabitants
during the reign of King Dasaratha

__data__.text: In Ayodhya, everyone maintained sacred fires and performed sacrifices. No one was petty, thieving, or immoral.  Brahmins were dedicated to their duties, controlled their senses, studied diligently, and were reluctant to accept gifts.  No one was unbelieving, dishonest, or ignorant of scriptures. Everyone was knowledgeable and generous.  All citizens had mastered the six branches of Vedic knowledge and observed pious vows. There was no misery or suffering.  Every man and woman was prosperous and loyal to the king.  People of all social classes worshipped the gods and guests. They were grateful, generous, heroic, and strong. All residents enjoyed long lives, devoted to virtue and truth, surrounded by their families.  Kshatriyas followed the Brahmins, Vaishyas followed the Kshatriyas, and Shudras focused on their work while serving the other three classes.  King Dasaratha protected Ayodhya as Manu had done before him.  The city was filled with skilled warriors, like a cave full of lions. It teemed with excellent horses from various regions.  Mighty elephants from the Vindhya and Himalayan mountains also filled the city.  Ayodhya, truly living up to its name of "unassailable," was vast and prosperous, and from its heart, King Dasaratha ruled his kingdom.  Like the moon governing the stars, the glorious King Dasaratha, having vanquished his foes, reigned over Ayodhya.

Canto VI

A description of the city of Ayodhya and the prosperity of its inhabitants

__data__.text: The grand city of Ayodhya teemed with various breeds of elephants, including those descended from the esteemed Airavata, Mahapadma, Ajana, and Vamana lineages, as well as those from the Bhadra, Mandra, and Mrga families.  The city's name, meaning "unassailable," was fitting, especially for its central area spanning two yojanas (sixteen miles).  From this central part of Ayodhya, King Dasaratha governed his kingdom. Just as the moon commands the stars, the illustrious King Dasaratha, having conquered his adversaries, presided over this splendid city. The city was adorned with diverse architecture, fortified by robust gates and bars, and populated by thousands.  King Dasaratha, comparable to Indra, ruled this auspicious city of Ayodhya.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto VII

The virtue and upright conduct of the eight ministers
of Emperor Dasaratha

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, a descendant of Ikswaku, was served by eight wise ministers. These ministers were skilled in judgment, understood others' intentions, and were deeply devoted to the king's welfare.  The eight ministers—Dhrsti, Jayanta, Vijaya, Surastra, Rastravardhana, Akopa, Dharmapala, and Sumantra—were renowned for their noble character and dedication to the affairs of state.  The king also relied on the counsel of esteemed priests and advisors, including Vasistha, Vamadeva, Suyajna, Jabali, Kasyapa, Gautama, Markandeya, and Katyayana. These advisors were learned, modest, and skilled, possessing self-control, wealth, and courage. They were known for their honesty, vigilance, and mastery of weaponry. They spoke kindly and truthfully, even when angered or tempted by wealth or pleasure.  Through a network of informants, they maintained a comprehensive understanding of events within the kingdom. They were diplomatic, loyal, and impartial in administering justice, even to their own families.  They prioritized the kingdom's financial stability and military strength, refraining from persecuting the innocent. These heroic ministers adhered to principles of just governance and diligently protected the well-behaved citizens of the kingdom.  By avoiding excessive taxation of the Brahmana and Kshatriya classes, and by imposing fair penalties based on the offender's resources and the severity of the crime, they ensured a prosperous and just kingdom. Their wisdom and integrity fostered a culture of honesty throughout Ayodhya and the surrounding kingdom, resulting in a peaceful and harmonious society free from wickedness and infidelity.

metadata.canto_summary:

__data__.text: King Dasaratha's eight ministers were valiant and dedicated to political science. They ensured the kingdom's prosperity by fairly taxing its citizens, considering both the severity of offenses and the offenders' resources when imposing penalties.  Due to their integrity and discernment, honesty prevailed throughout Ayodhya and the surrounding kingdom, eliminating wickedness and infidelity. The ministers dressed well, focused on the king's welfare, and were known for their wisdom and prowess, even in foreign lands. Skilled in diplomacy and warfare, they possessed inherent virtues and maintained confidentiality.  These ministers supported King Dasaratha's just rule over the kingdom. The king, renowned for his generosity and truthfulness, governed righteously, protected his subjects, and avoided injustice.  His unmatched strength, combined with the support of allies and the respect of neighboring rulers, allowed him to reign supreme, free from formidable enemies. Supported by his loyal and skilled counselors, King Dasaratha radiated splendor like the rising sun.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, a righteous and noble ruler, grieved over his lack of an heir.  He pondered how to solve this and decided a horse-sacrifice was the answer. After consulting with his wise counselors, he summoned his preceptors and priests, including the esteemed Vasistha.  The king expressed his sorrow and announced his intention to perform the horse-sacrifice to obtain a son.  He requested their guidance on the proper scriptural rites.  The Brahmanas, led by Vasistha, praised the king's plan. They advised him to gather the necessary materials, release the sacrificial horse, and prepare a sacrificial ground on the Sarayu's northern bank, assuring him he would obtain his desired sons.  Overjoyed, King Dasaratha instructed his ministers to follow the preceptors' instructions, ensuring the horse's release under competent guards and the preparation of the sacrificial ground.

He then addressed his wives, informing them of the upcoming sacrifice and their role in the consecration ceremony.  The queens rejoiced at the prospect.

__data__.text: Sumantra, a minister and charioteer to King Dasaratha, advises the king about the proposed horse-sacrifice.  He recounts a legend, also heard by him during a Puranic exposition, foretold by the sage Sanatkumara regarding the king's future sons. The story involves a sage named Vibhandaka and his son, Rsyasrnga, raised in the forest, unaware of the world beyond his father and dedicated to his religious duties.  During Rsyasrnga’s youth, King Romapada of Anga will face a severe drought.  Upon consulting Brahmanas, Romapada is advised to bring Rsyasrnga to his kingdom and offer his daughter, Santa, in marriage to him. This act will end the drought.  King Romapada then contemplates how to bring the secluded sage to his kingdom.

Canto IX

__data__.text: King Dasaratha is being advised by Sumantra about a prophecy regarding his future sons.  The Brahmanas advise King Romapada of Anga to bring Rsyasrnga, son of Vibhandaka, to end a drought by offering his daughter, Santa, in marriage to the sage.  Romapada ponders how to achieve this.  The king consults with his advisors and decides to send his priest and ministers to retrieve Rsyasrnga.  However, the priest and ministers express fear of Vibhandaka's potential curse and hesitate to go, promising to devise an alternative plan.  They later suggest a strategy to bring Rsyasrnga without incurring blame, involving courtesans and timed to coincide with the return of rain.  Sumantra concludes the prophecy by stating that Rsyasrnga, as Dasaratha's future son-in-law, will perform rituals that will grant Dasaratha sons.  Intrigued, Dasaratha asks Sumantra to elaborate on how Rsyasrnga was ultimately brought to Romapada.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha urges Sumantra to explain how Rsyasrnga was brought to Romapada's capital. Sumantra recounts the advice of Romapada's priest: aware of Rsyasrnga's isolated life dedicated to ascetic practices and Vedic studies, and his unfamiliarity with worldly pleasures and women, the priest suggested sending attractive courtesans to lure the sage from his forest abode. Romapada agreed, and the courtesans were dispatched.  Upon arriving near Vibhandaka's hermitage, the courtesans attempted to attract Rsyasrnga's attention.  One day, Rsyasrnga encountered the women, who were adorned in various colorful attire. They sang melodiously and inquired about his identity and solitary life. Having never encountered women before, Rsyasrnga was captivated by their beauty and felt compelled to reveal his name and parentage, inviting them to his nearby hermitage for proper hospitality.

metadata.canto_summary:

How the sage Rsyasrnga was lured away to the capital of Romapada

__data__.text: Rsyasrnga, encountering the courtesans for the first time, was captivated by their appearance.  He revealed his identity and invited them to his hermitage.  Upon their arrival, he offered them basic hospitality of water and forest fruits. The courtesans, anxious about Vibhandaka's return, readily accepted the hospitality but quickly decided to leave.  They offered Rsyasrnga special fruits and sweets before departing. Unaware of their true nature, Rsyasrnga accepted the gifts, believing them to be ordinary forest fare. After they left,  he felt a strange unease and longing. The following day, Rsyasrnga went in search of the women, finding them in the same area as before.  They greeted him warmly and invited him to their own supposed hermitage, promising even more delightful refreshments.

__data__.text: Rsyasrnga, still thinking about the women he met, returned to the same location. The courtesans, happy to see him, invited him to their supposed hermitage, promising even better food and drinks than he had received before.  Rsyasrnga, captivated by their offer, agreed to go with them. They led him to a boat on the Ganga River, and as they travelled towards the kingdom of Anga, a sudden rain shower brought joy to everyone. King Romapada, recognizing the auspicious timing of Rsyasrnga’s arrival with the rain, greeted him respectfully and offered him gifts. He then requested a boon from Rsyasrnga, asking forgiveness for luring him away from his father’s hermitage.  The King introduced Rsyasrnga to his adopted daughter, Santa, and they were married. Rsyasrnga, now content and well-provided for, lived with his new wife in the King's palace.

__data__.text: A celestial sage, Sanatkumara, prophesied that King Dasaratha, a righteous ruler of the Ikshvaku dynasty, would befriend the King of Anga, Romapada.  Romapada had a daughter, Santa, who was married to the sage Rsyasrnga.  Dasaratha, desiring an heir, would request Romapada to allow Rsyasrnga to perform a sacrifice for him. Romapada, after consideration, would agree to part with Rsyasrnga.  King Dasaratha, relieved and joyful, would then perform the sacrifice guided by Rsyasrnga.  Further, Dasaratha would respectfully request Rsyasrnga to officiate as the priest for the ceremony, hoping to gain both sons and a place in heaven. The prophecy foretold that the sacrifice would grant Dasaratha four powerful sons who would bring glory to their lineage.  Therefore, Sanatkumara advised the king to personally escort Rsyasrnga with appropriate honors and a retinue.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, eager to have a son, was advised to personally escort the sage Rsyasrnga back to his kingdom.  Heeding this advice, Dasaratha journeyed with his entourage to where Rsyasrnga resided with his wife, Santa, and Romapada.  Upon arrival, Dasaratha was warmly welcomed by Romapada, who then informed Rsyasrnga of his relationship with Dasaratha, including how Santa, originally Dasaratha’s daughter, came to be adopted by Romapada.  After a stay of several days, Dasaratha requested Rsyasrnga and Santa to return with him to his capital for an important undertaking.  Rsyasrnga agreed, and they departed together. Dasaratha sent messengers ahead to Ayodhya, instructing the citizens to prepare a grand welcome.  The city was decorated, and the citizens joyfully awaited the king’s arrival.  Dasaratha entered the city, leading the procession with Rsyasrnga at the forefront, amidst the sounds of conches and drums.  The citizens were delighted to witness the respect shown to the sage.  Dasaratha then brought Rsyasrnga and Santa into the palace, where they were warmly received by the queens. Santa, now with a child, brought great joy to everyone, and she and her family lived happily in the palace for a time.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, desiring a son, decided to perform a horse-sacrifice after a long time had passed since Rishyasringa's arrival and the arrival of a beautiful spring. He respectfully requested Rishyasringa, a brahmin with divine radiance, to lead the sacrifice for the continuation of his lineage. Rishyasringa agreed and instructed the king to gather the necessary materials, release the horse, and prepare the sacrificial ground on Sarayu's northern bank. The king then commanded Sumantra to quickly summon learned Brahmins and Vedic scholars, including Suyajna, Vamadeva, Jabali, Kashyapa, and his family priest Vasistha. Sumantra promptly gathered the Brahmins, and King Dasaratha reverently addressed them, expressing his sorrow over not having a son.  He declared his intention to perform the Ashwamedha sacrifice, believing that through Rishyasringa's spiritual power, he would achieve his desires. The Brahmins approved, echoing Rishyasringa's instructions and assuring the king that he would gain four powerful sons.  Overjoyed, the king ordered his ministers to swiftly gather the necessary resources and release the sacrificial horse under the protection of four hundred capable Kshatriya princes and the chief priest.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, pleased with the Brahmins' assurances of sons, instructed his ministers to swiftly gather the necessary materials for the horse-sacrifice. He ordered the release of the sacrificial horse under the protection of four hundred Kshatriya princes and the chief priest.  The king emphasized the importance of preparing the sacrificial ground on the northern bank of the Sarayu river and conducting all propitiatory rites according to the scriptures.  He stressed the need for meticulous adherence to the prescribed procedures, warning that even minor transgressions during such a significant sacrifice could lead to its failure and dire consequences. The king, confident in his ministers' abilities, urged them to ensure the flawless execution of the ritual. The ministers readily agreed to carry out the king's commands.  After the Brahmins departed, the king dismissed his ministers and retired to his private chambers.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, having completed one year since his discussion with Vasistha, again approached the sage to initiate the horse-sacrifice meant to bless him with a son.  He respectfully greeted Vasistha and Arundhati, his wife, and humbly requested the sage to oversee the ceremony, ensuring it adhered to sacred scriptures and remained protected from any disruptions.  Dasaratha acknowledged Vasistha as his trusted advisor and spiritual guide, entrusting him with the entire responsibility of the elaborate ritual. Vasistha agreed to the king's request. He then directed the elderly Brahmanas learned in rituals and architecture, along with other skilled individuals like artists, carpenters, excavators, astronomers, artisans, and even actors and dancers to commence their respective duties for the sacrifice.  He also instructed the gathering of a large quantity of bricks for the construction of royal palaces, well-stocked with provisions.  Vasistha emphasized the need for comfortable accommodations for the Brahmanas, replete with food and drinks, and also ordered the construction of spacious housing and wells for the citizens, separate mansions for visiting royalty, stables for horses, stalls for elephants, and ample housing for warriors both local and from other kingdoms.  He stressed the importance of generously providing food and hospitality to everyone, ensuring all guests, regardless of their social standing, felt honored and respected.

__data__.text: Vasistha continued to meticulously plan the arrangements for the grand horse-sacrifice. He instructed that stables be constructed for royal guests' horses and elephants, along with comfortable lodging for warriors from both within and outside the kingdom, ensuring they were well-provisioned.  He also ordered that ample food and other necessities be provided to citizens and visitors alike, with a special emphasis on treating everyone with respect and honor, regardless of their social standing, and avoiding any discourtesy even amidst the pressures of organizing the event.  He instructed those in charge to look after the well-being of all those involved in the sacrificial preparations, especially the Brahmanas and artisans, ensuring they were duly compensated and honored.  The assembled officials assured Vasistha that his instructions would be carried out without fail.  Following this, Vasistha instructed Sumantra, the king's trusted minister, to extend invitations to pious individuals from all walks of life and from all corners of the earth, including Brahmanas, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, and Shudras.  He specifically emphasized the invitation to King Janaka of Mithila, highlighting him as a potential relative through the future marriage alliances of Dasaratha’s sons. He also urged the invitation of other prominent rulers, such as the virtuous king of Kashi, the elderly King of Kekayas (Dasaratha's father-in-law), King Romapada of Anga (Dasaratha's friend), King Bhanuman of Kosala, the learned King PraptijAa of Magadha, and the rulers of the eastern territories, Sindhusauvira, and Saurastra.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha commenced a grand horse-sacrifice on the northern bank of the Sarayu River.  Led by the sage Rsyasrnga, learned Brahmanas expertly performed the various rites, adhering strictly to Vedic scriptures and procedures. They diligently completed the Pravargya and Upasada rituals, and offered worship to the gods with reverence.  The priests carried out the morning, midday, and third Savana offerings, including the oblation to Indra. Rsyasrnga and other priests invoked the gods with precisely pronounced incantations and offered them appropriate oblations. The sacrifice proceeded flawlessly, with no errors or omissions.  Throughout the ceremony, the priests remained tireless and well-nourished.  Numerous Brahmanas, ascetics, and even women and children were generously fed delicious food, and clothing was distributed abundantly.  Mountains of cooked rice were prepared daily for the attendees.

__data__.text: The Brahmanas, other twice-born castes, servants, ascetics, students, retired forest dwellers, recluses, the elderly, the sick, women, and children all partook in the continuous feasting, enjoying the delicious food provided at the sacrifice.  Generous donations of food and clothing were distributed to all attendees.  Mountains of cooked rice were prepared daily. People from various regions, both men and women, were warmly welcomed and hosted at King Dasaratha's magnificent sacrificial ceremony. The Brahmanas praised the properly prepared, flavorful food, expressing their satisfaction to the king.  Adorned servers attended to the Brahmanas, while others wearing polished gem earrings assisted the servers.  Between ritual portions of the ceremony, learned Brahmanas engaged in lively discussions, presenting reasoned arguments. Expert Brahmanas diligently performed all the rites according to sacred scriptures, guided by their elders. The officiating priests were all knowledgeable in the six branches of Vedic studies, observant of sacred vows, and skilled in debate.  When the time came, twenty-one pillars—six of Bilva wood, six of Khadira wood paired with the Bilva pillars, six of Palasa wood, one of Slesmataka wood, and two of Deodar cedar spaced six feet apart—were erected for the sacrifice.  These gold-plated pillars, each twenty-one spans high and covered with cloth, had been meticulously crafted by skilled carpenters according to scriptural guidelines.  Draped in cloth, adorned with flowers, and fragrant with sandalwood paste, they shone brightly like the constellation of the Great Bear in the night sky.

__data__.text: Preparations for the horse sacrifice were meticulously completed under the guidance of experts versed in scripture and ritual.  Twenty-one gold-plated pillars, each twenty-one spans high, were erected and adorned with cloth.  Skilled carpenters had crafted these octagonal pillars with smooth surfaces, adhering to scriptural guidelines.  Decorated with cloth, flowers, and sandalwood paste, they resembled the radiant constellation of the Great Bear.  Bricks for the sacrificial altar were made to precise scriptural measurements by skilled priests.  The altar's fire pit, designed for King Dasaratha, was constructed in the shape of Garuda and was three times larger than typical altars.  Animals, including serpents and birds, were bound to the pillars as offerings to specific deities, following scriptural instructions. The sacrificial horse and aquatic creatures were also secured, adhering to sacred ordinances. Three hundred animals, including King Dasaratha's finest horse, were tied to the pillars. Queen Kausalya and the other queens ceremonially touched the horse with swords, and Kausalya spent a night with it for religious merit.  The four chief priests oversaw the ritual contact between the horse and the king's wives. The chief priest, a master of ritual, prepared the Aswakanda tuber according to scripture, and the king inhaled its steam to purify himself.  Sixteen priests then offered the prescribed portions of the horse into the sacrificial fire, placing the offerings on cane mats as dictated by the Aswamedha ritual.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XIV

King Dasaratha proceeds with the horse-sacrifice; RsyaSrnga grants
him a boon regarding the birth of four sons

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, having inhaled the purifying steam of the Aswakanda tuber, continued with the horse sacrifice.  The sixteen officiating priests offered portions of the horse into the sacred fire, placing the offerings on cane mats, a specific requirement of the Aswamedha ritual.  This elaborate sacrifice included three days dedicated to the Soma ritual, with specific names for each day's ceremony: Catustoma (Jyotistoma) for the first, Ukthya for the second, and Atiratra for the third.  Several supplementary rituals were also performed.  Among these were the Jyotistoma and Ayustoma, two rounds of Atiratra, Abhijit, ViSwajit, and two rounds of Aptoryama—a total of eight significant sacrifices.  Following the completion of the sacrifice, King Dasaratha, as dictated by tradition, offered the priests the four quarters of the earth as a sacrificial fee: east to the Hota, west to the Adhwaryu, south to the Brahma, and north to the Udgata.  Overjoyed with the successful completion, the king presented this symbolic gift. However, the priests, devoted to their Vedic studies, declined the land, explaining they were unable to govern it and preferred a more practical offering of gems, gold, cows, or anything readily usable.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, having symbolically offered the earth to the priests for their services, was met with a polite refusal.  The learned Brahmanas, devoted to their Vedic studies, requested a more practical reward of gold, gems, or cattle.  The king generously granted their wish, bestowing upon them vast riches: ten lakhs (one million) cows, one hundred million gold coins, and four times that amount in silver.  The priests, in turn, entrusted these riches to the sages Rishyashringa and Vasistha for fair distribution.  Pleased with this arrangement, the Brahmanas expressed their satisfaction. Dasaratha continued his generosity, gifting ten million gold coins to visiting Brahmanas.  His treasury depleted, he even offered his own bangle to a needy Brahmin who requested aid.  With the Brahmanas satisfied, a joyful Dasaratha, overcome with emotion, bowed before them.  They, in turn, showered him with blessings as he lay prostrate.  Having successfully completed the arduous and purifying horse sacrifice, a ritual rarely achieved even by the greatest kings, Dasaratha felt immense joy. He then turned to Rishyashringa, requesting the sage to perform further rites that would ensure the continuation of his lineage.  Rishyashringa agreed, promising the king four sons who would carry on his legacy. Overjoyed by this promise, Dasaratha, full of pious devotion, bowed again to Rishyashringa and repeated his earnest request.

__data__.text: Rsyasrnga, a skilled Vedic scholar, informed King Dasaratha that he would perform a Putra Kameshti Yagna, a specific ritual from the Atharva Veda designed to ensure the birth of sons.  He then commenced the elaborate ceremony, offering oblations into the sacred fire. Gods, Gandharvas, Siddhas, and revered seers gathered to receive their respective shares of the offerings.  Unseen by mortals, the deities approached Brahma and voiced their concerns about Ravana.  They explained how Ravana, empowered by a boon granted by Brahma, tormented them and disrupted the three worlds. They highlighted Ravana's disdain for everyone, including Indra, and emphasized his invincibility against natural forces. Fearful of this formidable foe, the gods pleaded with Brahma to find a way to eliminate him. After contemplating their request, Brahma revealed that he had devised a plan.  He recalled Ravana's request for immunity from Gandharvas, Yakshas, gods, and demons, but noted that Ravana had, out of arrogance, omitted humans from his request. Thus, Brahma declared, Ravana's death could only be brought about by a human.  This pronouncement brought immense joy to the assembled gods and Rsis.

__data__.text: Brahma, after considering the gods' plea, reveals a strategy for Ravana's demise. He reminds them of Ravana's arrogance in requesting immunity from celestial beings but overlooking humans. This oversight makes Ravana vulnerable to a human, his sole means of destruction. The gods and Rishis rejoice at this revelation.  Meanwhile, Vishnu, adorned in gold and wielding his divine weapons, arrives on Garuda. He joins the assembly where the gods beseech him to intervene. They request Vishnu to incarnate as Dasaratha's son to rid the world of Ravana. They detail Ravana's tyranny against gods, Rishis, and celestial beings, emphasizing his invincibility against any being other than a human. The gods implore Vishnu, their ultimate protector, to descend to the mortal realm and eliminate Ravana, the enemy of the gods.  Vishnu, revered by all, then addresses the assembled deities led by Brahma.

__data__.text: Vishnu, approached by the gods, is asked to intervene and vanquish Ravana.  They propose that he be born as the son of King Dasaratha of Ayodhya, a virtuous and powerful king.  The gods lament Ravana's oppression of them, the Gandharvas, Siddhas, and Rishis, highlighting his displacement of celestial beings from Nandana Vana. They appeal to Vishnu for protection and salvation, emphasizing his role as their ultimate refuge.  In response, Vishnu assures the gods of his intervention, promising to eliminate Ravana and his lineage. He commits to dwelling on Earth for eleven thousand years.  Vishnu chooses King Dasaratha as his earthly father and is praised by the gods, Rishis, Gandharvas, Rudra, and Apsaras for his divine attributes.  They again implore him to destroy Ravana, emphasizing Ravana's arrogance, hostility towards Indra, and torment of ascetics and hermits. They request Vishnu's return to his celestial abode after completing this task.

__data__.text: The gods, concerned about Ravana's tyranny, asked Lord Vishnu for a solution to defeat him. Vishnu inquired about a strategy, knowing Ravana's strength. The gods suggested that Vishnu take a human form to overcome Ravana, revealing that Ravana had received a boon from Brahma, granting him immunity from all beings except humans.  Due to this, Ravana was causing widespread destruction and abducting women.  Upon hearing this, Vishnu chose King Dasaratha to be his father. Dasaratha, desiring a son, performed a sacrifice. After bidding farewell to Brahma, Vishnu vanished. A powerful being then emerged from the sacrificial fire, possessing a striking appearance with dark skin, red attire, and a booming voice.  He carried a golden basin filled with a sweet rice pudding.

__data__.text: A divine messenger, emanating a fiery aura, emerged from the sacrificial fire.  His skin was dark, his clothes red, and his voice resonated like a kettledrum. He was adorned with celestial jewels and auspicious markings, his stature mountainous and his stride powerful, like a tiger.  He carried a golden vessel, brimming with a divine rice pudding and covered with a silver lid, as preciously as a beloved spouse.  Addressing King Dasaratha, the messenger identified himself as a representative of Lord Vishnu.  Dasaratha welcomed the divine being and inquired how he could be of service. The messenger explained that the king's sacrifices had earned him this reward—the pudding, a gift from the gods, capable of granting sons, wealth, and health. He instructed Dasaratha to share it with his wives, so they might bear the sons for whom he had performed the rituals.  Dasaratha gratefully accepted the offering and respectfully circled the messenger. Overjoyed, the king carried the pudding to his wives' quarters, which seemed to glow with his happiness. He presented half of the pudding to his chief wife, Kausalya, telling her it would grant her a son.  He then gave half of the remaining portion to his second wife, Sumitra, for the same purpose.

__data__.text: After fulfilling his divine duty, the radiant messenger vanished back into the sacrificial fire. King Dasaratha, beaming with joy, returned to his palace, his happiness illuminating the quarters like the autumn moon.  He presented a portion of the divine pudding to Kausalya, his chief wife, explaining it would grant her a son.  He then gave half of the remaining portion to Sumitra.  Next, he offered half of what remained to Kaikeyi.  After a moment of thought, Dasaratha gave the remaining portion to Sumitra as well. Thus, he distributed the pudding among his wives, each receiving a share. The queens felt honored and overjoyed by this gift, believing it would bring them the blessing of children.  Upon consuming the pudding, they soon sensed the presence of powerful beings within their wombs, radiating with an energy comparable to the sun and fire. Witnessing his wives now carrying his future children, King Dasaratha felt immense joy and fulfillment, akin to Indra, king of the gods, surrounded by celestial beings.

__data__.text:
Brahma, the self-born creator, commanded the gods to father powerful offspring who could shapeshift and aid the virtuous Lord Vishnu. These sons would be swift, intelligent, and as strong as Vishnu himself, skilled in warfare and impossible to kill.  They would be born through celestial nymphs, Gandharva women, Yaksha and Naga maidens, she-bears, Vidyadhara women, Kinnaras, and she-monkeys.

Brahma declared he had already created Jambavan, the bear chief. The gods obeyed Brahma's decree and began to father their monkey sons.  Even the high-souled Rishis, Siddhas, Vidyadharas, Nagas, and Caranas sired heroic monkey offspring.  Indra, king of the gods, fathered Vali, a monkey king who rivaled his own strength. The sun god begot Sugriva, Vali's younger brother.  Brihaspati, the preceptor of the gods, fathered the wise Tara.  Kubera sired Gandhamadana, while Viswakarma, the divine architect, begot Nala. The fire god’s son, Nila, shone brightly like fire itself, surpassing all in glory and might. The Aswins, known for their beauty, fathered Mainda and Dwivida.  Varuna, the water god, begot Susena, while Parjanya, the rain god, fathered Sarabha.

__data__.text: Following Brahma's command, many powerful monkey sons were born to various deities.  Kubera fathered Gandhamadana, and Viswakarma, Nala.  Nila, son of the fire god, was exceptionally radiant and strong. The Aswins fathered Mainda and Dwivida. Varuna fathered Susena, and Parjanya, Sarabha.  Hanuman, son of the wind god, possessed immense strength and speed, rivaling Garuda. These monkey chiefs, numbering in the thousands, were intelligent, strong, and ready to defeat Ravana.  They could change their shape at will, possessed immense strength, and were as large as mountains.  These monkeys, bears, and those with cow-like tails were quickly born, each inheriting the traits of their divine fathers. Those born to she-monkeys displayed even greater prowess. Gods, Rishis, Gandharvas, Yakshas, Nagas, Kinnaras, Vidyadharas, and Siddhas all fathered monkey sons, some through Apsaras, Vidyadhara women, Naga maidens, and Gandharva women.  These sons were powerful, roaming the forests, and able to assume any form and strength.  They were proud and strong like lions and tigers, using rocks and mountains as weapons. Their claws and teeth were formidable, and they were skilled with all sorts of missiles, capable of shaking mountains, splitting trees, stirring the sea, and leaping across vast oceans.

They roamed the forests, resembling lions and tigers in their pride and power, wielding rocks and mountains as weapons. Their claws and teeth were formidable, complemented by their skill with all manner of missiles. They could shake mountains, shatter trees, churn the sea, and leap across vast oceans.  They commanded the skies, seizing clouds and even powerful elephants.  Their roars could bring down birds in flight.  These diverse monkey forces, numbering in the millions, gathered under prominent leaders like Sugriva and Vali, ready to serve Rama. Vali, with his exceptional strength, protected the bears, monkeys, and other creatures under his command.  The earth teemed with these formidable warriors, their varied forms and markings a testament to their diverse origins, all united in their purpose to aid Rama.

__data__.text: After King Dasaratha's sacrifices concluded, the attending deities departed. The king returned to Ayodhya with his entourage.  Honored by Dasaratha, the assembled kings returned to their kingdoms.  Dasaratha then re-entered Ayodhya, honoring the Brahmins.  Rishyasringa also departed with his wife, Santa, after being respectfully escorted by the king. Content with fulfilling his ambitions, Dasaratha eagerly anticipated the birth of sons.  Time passed, and under auspicious astrological alignments, Queen Kausalya gave birth to Rama, a glorious son possessing divine qualities.  Queen Kaikeyi gave birth to Bharata, a valiant and virtuous son. Queen Sumitra gave birth to twins, Lakshmana and Shatrughna, both skilled warriors.  The births of Bharata and the twins also occurred under specific astrological conditions.

__data__.text: Following the joyous births of Dasaratha's sons,  Kausalya radiated with pride for her glorious son, Rama.  Bharata, born to Kaikeyi, possessed great valor. Sumitra's twins, Lakshmana and Shatrughna, were both skilled warriors.  Bharata was born under the constellation Pusya, while Lakshmana and Shatrughna were born under Ashlesha.  The four brothers, though born separately, shared a similar noble nature and impressive virtues.  Celestial beings celebrated their births with music, dance, and flower showers.  Ayodhya rejoiced with festivities, the streets filled with performers and crowds. The king generously rewarded entertainers and Brahmins.  Eleven days after the twins' birth, a naming ceremony was held.  Vasistha named the princes Rama, Bharata, Lakshmana, and Shatrughna.  Dasaratha ensured all customary rites were performed for his sons. Rama, the eldest, was a source of great joy for his father and a shining example of their lineage.  All four princes were learned in the Vedas, courageous, and dedicated to the welfare of the people. While all possessed wisdom and virtue, Rama was particularly renowned for his prowess. He was admired by all and skilled in various forms of combat and charioteering.

__data__.text: Dasaratha oversaw all the necessary rites for his sons, from birth to their investiture with the sacred thread. Rama, the eldest, brought immense joy to his father and exemplified their lineage's greatness. All four princes were learned in the Vedas, courageous, and committed to the people's welfare.  While all were wise and virtuous, Rama's prowess stood out, earning him widespread admiration for his combat and charioteering skills. He was beloved by all, like the full moon in a clear sky.  Lakshmana was deeply devoted to Rama, serving as his constant companion and protector.  Similarly, Shatrughna was inseparable from Bharata, and they shared a deep bond of affection. Dasaratha was overjoyed with his four sons, feeling blessed like Brahma with the gods. As the princes matured, they displayed wisdom, virtue, modesty, knowledge, and foresight. Dasaratha, proud of their unique glory and splendor, rejoiced.  The princes diligently pursued their Vedic studies, served their parents, and mastered archery.  As their education neared completion, Dasaratha consulted with priests and family about their marriages. During these deliberations, the renowned sage Viswamitra arrived at the palace.  Viswamitra was greeted with utmost respect by Dasaratha, who considered the sage's visit a blessing and an honor. Dasaratha expressed his readiness to fulfill any request the sage might have.  Viswamitra, pleased by the king's respectful words, felt great joy.

__data__.text: Dasaratha's son Lakshmana was deeply devoted to Rama, constantly by his side.  Lakshmana was so essential to Rama's well-being that Rama neither slept nor ate without him.  When Rama went hunting, Lakshmana followed, armed and ready to protect him.  Similarly, Shatrughna was inseparable from Bharata, their bond as strong as Lakshmana's with Rama.  King Dasaratha was immensely pleased with his four sons, feeling as blessed as Brahma with the gods.  The princes grew in wisdom, virtue, and knowledge.  Dasaratha, filled with pride, rejoiced in their accomplishments.  The princes dedicated themselves to their studies, served their parents, and mastered archery. As their education neared its end, Dasaratha began to consider their marriages, consulting with priests and family. During these discussions, the revered sage Viswamitra arrived at the palace.  Dasaratha, honored by the sage's presence, offered him the utmost respect and eagerly awaited his request. Viswamitra, pleased with the king's reception, inquired about the welfare of the kingdom and the king's family, hoping all was well and that Dasaratha's duties were being fulfilled.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha warmly greeted Sage Viswamitra, expressing his joy at the sage's arrival.  He compared the sage's visit to finding nectar, receiving rain after a drought, the birth of a son, recovering lost treasure, and the joy of a festival. Dasaratha felt blessed and honored, declaring that Viswamitra's presence had purified him.  He eagerly asked the sage the purpose of his visit, promising to fulfill any request and offer any assistance. Dasaratha felt the sage's presence brought great joy and religious merit to him and his kingdom.  Viswamitra was pleased by the king's respectful and heartfelt words.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, responding to King Dasaratha's lengthy speech, assures the king that his words are fitting for a descendant of a noble lineage.  He then requests Dasaratha to fulfill his urgent purpose by allowing Rama to accompany him.  Viswamitra explains that his sacrificial rites are being disrupted by two powerful, shape-shifting demons, Marica and Subahu, who defiled his altar with blood and flesh.  Due to the sacred nature of the rites, Viswamitra could not retaliate with a curse. He believes that only Rama, despite his youth, possesses the necessary prowess to defeat these demons, especially with Viswamitra's protection and the boons he will bestow upon him.  Viswamitra emphasizes Rama's unique capability and guarantees their demise, stating that they are no match for Rama's power and are already ensnared by fate. He urges Dasaratha not to let parental affection interfere with this necessary action.

__data__.text: Viswamitra reiterates that only Rama possesses the strength and destiny to vanquish the demons, Marica and Subahu. He assures Dasaratha that the demons' demise is certain, given Rama's prowess.  Viswamitra then appeals to Dasaratha's sense of duty and righteousness, urging him to allow Rama to accompany him for ten days and nights to protect the sacrificial rites.  He advises Dasaratha to consult with his counselors, led by Vasistha, and to prioritize religious merit and renown over his paternal affection.  Upon hearing Viswamitra's plea, Dasaratha is overwhelmed with grief and faints.  He eventually regains consciousness but remains deeply distressed by the prospect of parting with Rama.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, deeply distressed by Viswamitra's request, argued against Rama's participation in the fight against the Rakshasas. He highlighted Rama's young age and lack of battle experience, emphasizing his inability to face such formidable foes.  Dasaratha offered his own army and personal involvement in the battle, proposing to protect Viswamitra's sacred rituals himself.  He reiterated his deep paternal concern, emphasizing the difficulty he faced in having Rama, his eldest and most beloved son, and pleaded with Viswamitra not to take him.  Dasaratha then inquired about the Rakshasas' strength, lineage, allies, and tactics, seeking guidance on how to best confront them in battle, whether with Rama, his army, or himself.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha continued to resist Viswamitra's request, declaring that Rama was no match for the treacherous Rakshasas.  He insisted that he could not bear to be separated from Rama and pleaded with Viswamitra not to take him. Dasaratha reiterated his offer to personally lead his army against the Rakshasas, emphasizing his long life and the difficulty he faced in having Rama, his most beloved son. He then inquired about the Rakshasas' lineage, allies, strength, and tactics, asking Viswamitra for guidance on the best course of action against these formidable foes.  He expressed his concern about facing the Rakshasas, highlighting their wicked intentions and formidable prowess.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha continued to argue against Viswamitra's request. He claimed that Ravana steals the strength of even the strongest warriors, making him an impossible opponent for Rama, even with the support of Dasaratha's army and other sons.  He firmly refused to send his young, war-inexperienced son Rama.  Dasaratha offered to personally lead his army against whichever of Maricha and Subahu, the powerful and skilled sons of Sunda and Upasunda, disrupted Viswamitra's sacrifices.  If neither was the culprit, he would still go with his army but asked for Viswamitra's understanding if he couldn't fulfill the request to send Rama.  This refusal and rambling angered Viswamitra greatly, like a sacrificial fire flaring up when fed with ghee.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha's hesitant reply, filled with fatherly love, enraged the sage Viswamitra. Viswamitra accused the king of breaking his promise, an act unworthy of the Raghu dynasty and one that would bring ruin upon them. He threatened to leave without Rama if the king did not honor his word. Viswamitra's anger shook the earth and alarmed the gods. Witnessing this, the wise sage Vasistha advised Dasaratha to uphold his promise, reminding him of his lineage and reputation for righteousness. He warned that breaking his word would negate the merit he had earned through sacrifices and good deeds. Vasistha assured the king that Rama would be safe under Viswamitra's protection, describing the sage as a powerful and learned ascetic, knowledgeable in various mystical weapons. He revealed that Viswamitra had been gifted these weapons by Lord Shiva, born as sons of Krsaswa, possessing diverse forms and immense power, ensuring victory for whoever wielded them.

in favour of ViSwamitra

__data__.text: Vasistha continued his praise of Viswamitra, emphasizing the sage's unique knowledge of mystical weapons. He explained that no being, divine or demonic, knew these weapons as Viswamitra did. These weapons, originally the pious sons of Krsaswa, were gifted to Viswamitra by Lord Shiva during his reign. Born of Daksa's daughters, Jaya and Suprabha, these weapons possessed diverse forms and immense power, guaranteeing victory for their wielder. Jaya bore fifty formless sons of immeasurable glory to vanquish demonic forces, while Suprabha bore fifty exceptionally powerful sons known as Samharas, difficult to overcome or even attack.  Vasistha highlighted Viswamitra's mastery of these weapons, his ability to create new ones, and his profound knowledge of dharma, making him omniscient. He urged Dasaratha to dismiss any reservations about sending Rama, assuring him that Viswamitra, despite his own power, sought Rama's assistance for the prince's benefit.  Reassured by Vasistha's words, Dasaratha, the renowned king of the Raghu dynasty, agreed to send Rama with Viswamitra.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, filled with joy, summoned Rama and Lakshmana.  After receiving blessings from their mother Kausalya, father Dasaratha, and the priest Vasistha, the princes were entrusted to Viswamitra's care.  As Rama followed Viswamitra, a refreshing breeze blew, and a shower of flowers fell from the sky, accompanied by the sounds of conches and drums. Viswamitra led the way, followed by Rama, armed with his bow, and then Lakshmana.  The two princes, radiating glory, resembled divine serpents as they accompanied the sage.  Adorned with jewels and carrying bows, swords, and protected by gloves, Rama and Lakshmana enhanced Viswamitra's presence. After traveling some distance along the Sarayu river's southern bank, Viswamitra advised Rama to drink some water and then offered to teach him the Bala and Atibala mantras, which would protect him from fatigue, illness, aging, and enemy attacks, granting him unparalleled strength.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, accompanied by Rama and Lakshmana, continued their journey.  The princes, adorned in jewels, armor, and carrying weapons, radiated an aura of power and grace.  They walked along the southern bank of the Sarayu river for about twelve miles. Viswamitra then instructed Rama to drink some water before initiating him and Lakshmana into the Bala and Atibala mantras.  These powerful spells, Viswamitra explained, would protect them from exhaustion, sickness, the effects of aging, and enemy attacks, making them virtually invincible. He emphasized that by mastering these mantras, they would surpass all others in strength, wisdom, and good fortune.  After receiving the mantras and their instructions, Rama and Lakshmana diligently performed their duties as students to their guru, Viswamitra.  The three then rested comfortably for the night on the banks of the Sarayu River, despite their simple bedding of straw.

__data__.text: Upon arriving at the confluence of the Ganga and Sarayu rivers at dawn, Viswamitra roused Rama and Lakshmana.  He urged them to rise and perform their morning rituals and prayers. The princes obeyed, completing their ablutions and offering water to the sun god before reciting the sacred Gayatri mantra.  After finishing their devotions, they greeted Viswamitra and eagerly awaited their next instruction. As they journeyed onward, they came upon the divine Ganga river and were awestruck by the holy hermitages of sages who had dedicated thousands of years to ascetic practices. Filled with reverence, Rama and Lakshmana inquired about the hermitage and its inhabitant.  Viswamitra, amused by their curiosity, shared the story of the hermitage's origin. He recounted how Kama, the god of love, foolishly tried to interrupt Lord Shiva's meditation with arrows of love. Enraged, Shiva destroyed Kama's physical form with a glance from his third eye, rendering him bodiless (Ananga). The land where this occurred became known as Anga. Viswamitra explained that the hermitage belonged to Shiva and the sages residing there were his former disciples, pure and devoid of sin.

__data__.text: Viswamitra finished explaining the origin of the hermitage and the surrounding region. He described how Lord Shiva, enraged by Kamadeva's attempt to interrupt his meditation, destroyed Kama's physical form with a glance from his third eye. This rendered Kamadeva bodiless (Ananga) and the area where this occurred became known as Anga. Viswamitra identified the hermitage as belonging to Shiva, and the sages residing there as Shiva's former disciples, pure and without sin.  He then suggested they spend the night at the hermitage situated between two holy rivers before crossing the Ganga the following day. The group proceeded to the hermitage. The sages residing there, having sensed their arrival through their ascetic powers, joyfully welcomed them. They offered Viswamitra, Rama, and Lakshmana water for washing and other hospitable gestures.  Viswamitra and the princes reciprocated these courtesies, and the sages entertained them with stories. As evening approached, everyone recited the Gayatri mantra.  Viswamitra, a respected ascetic, entertained Rama and Lakshmana with delightful tales as they rested comfortably in the hermitage.

__data__.text: As they crossed the Ganga River, Rama and Lakshmana heard a loud rushing sound. Curious, Rama asked sage Vishvamitra about the source of the commotion.  Vishvamitra explained that the sound originated from the Sarayu River, a sacred river flowing from a lake created by Brahma on Mount Kailasa. This river, he explained, encircles Ayodhya and joins the Ganga.  He advised the princes to pay their respects to the confluence of the two rivers.  After doing so, Rama, Lakshmana, and Vishvamitra continued their journey, reaching a dense and ominous forest.  Observing the unsettling environment filled with crickets, wild animals, and birds of prey, Rama, concerned, questioned Vishvamitra about the nature of this formidable forest.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XXIV

An account of the Sarayu given by ViSwamitra; some facts relating to the
