__data__.text: Sage Vishvamitra explained the origin of the Sarayu River to Rama and Lakshmana.  It began, he said, in a lake created by Brahma on Mount Kailasa and flowed past Ayodhya before joining the Ganga.  He urged the princes to show respect at the confluence of the two rivers.  Continuing their journey, they entered a dense, forbidding forest teeming with insects, predatory animals, and birds.  Rama, disturbed by the ominous atmosphere, asked Vishvamitra about the nature of this desolate place.  Vishvamitra described it as being filled with lions, tigers, boars, elephants, and a variety of trees. He then began to explain the history of the area, recounting how two prosperous kingdoms, Malada and Karusa, once flourished there.  He told of how Indra, burdened by the sin of killing a Brahmin, was cleansed by the gods and sages.  Indra's impurity and hunger were transferred to this land, which he then blessed, giving rise to the kingdoms.  However, over time, a powerful Yakshini named Tataka, wife of Sunda and mother of Maricha, came to inhabit the area.  Tataka, with the strength of a thousand elephants, terrorized the inhabitants and laid waste to the kingdoms. Vishvamitra then revealed that their purpose in entering this forest was to defeat Tataka and restore peace to the land.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XXIV

__data__.text: Vishvamitra continued his story, explaining how the once prosperous lands of Malada and Karusa, blessed by Indra, eventually fell into ruin. A powerful Yakshini named Tataka, wife of Sunda, arrived and began terrorizing the region.  She possessed immense strength and her son, Maricha, was equally formidable.  Tataka's reign of terror had devastated the two kingdoms.  Vishvamitra concluded his tale by revealing their mission: to slay the wicked Tataka and restore peace to the land. He urged Rama to use his strength to rid the region of her evil presence, highlighting the importance of their task by emphasizing how dangerous the area had become due to Tataka's unchecked power.

__data__.text: Viswamitra explains to Rama why Tataka, despite being female, possesses immense strength.  He recounts how Tataka's father, the Yaksha Suketu, performed austerities and was granted a daughter by Brahma. Brahma bestowed upon Tataka the strength of a thousand elephants.  Suketu then gave Tataka in marriage to Sunda, son of Jambha.  They had a son named Marica.  After Sunda's death, Tataka and Marica tried to attack Agastya, who cursed Marica to become a flesh-eating ogre and Tataka to become an ugly, man-eating ogress.  Enraged by the curse, Tataka now wreaks havoc on the land. Viswamitra urges Rama to kill Tataka for the well-being of the Brahmanas and the cows.

__data__.text: Viswamitra continues his exhortation to Rama, emphasizing the necessity of killing Tataka.  He describes how Tataka and her son, Marica, attacked Agastya.  Agastya cursed Marica to become an ogre and Tataka to become a hideous, man-eating ogress.  Now enraged and empowered by the curse, she terrorizes the region formerly sanctified by Agastya’s presence. Viswamitra implores Rama to slay Tataka for the benefit of the Brahmanas and cows, stressing that only Rama possesses the strength to overcome her curse-enhanced power. He dispels any reservations Rama might have about killing a woman, arguing that a Kshatriya prince must perform even unpleasant duties for the greater good of society.  Viswamitra cites precedents of powerful figures like Indra and Vishnu who have killed women when necessary to protect the world.  He concludes by urging Rama to overcome his compassion and fulfill his duty by killing the wicked Tataka.

Canto XXVI

__data__.text: Rama, resolute, responded to Viswamitra's call to action, stating his duty to obey both his father and the sage.  He declared his intent to kill Tataka for the benefit of the Brahmins, the cows, the region, and to honor Viswamitra.  Rama then readied his bow, creating a loud twang that startled the forest and enraged Tataka.  She, infuriated, charged towards the source of the sound.  Rama, observing Tataka's monstrous form, pointed her out to Lakshmana, commenting on her fearsome appearance. He declared his intention to disable, rather than kill, the ogress, depriving her of her ears, nose, hands, and feet. As Rama spoke, Tataka, in a fit of rage, lunged at him. Viswamitra, intervening with protective chants, wished the brothers well and shouted a cry of victory.  Tataka then kicked up a massive dust cloud, momentarily obscuring the brothers' vision.

metadata.canto_summary:

Sri Rama kills Tataka

Canto XXVI

Sri Rama kills Tataka

__data__.text: Rama, hesitant to kill a woman, decided to cripple Tataka instead, aiming to remove her hands and feet.  As he spoke, the enraged Tataka charged towards him. Viswamitra blessed the brothers, shouting a cry of victory.  Tataka then created a massive dust cloud, briefly blinding Rama and Lakshmana.  Resorting to magic, she then bombarded them with rocks.  Angered, Rama retaliated with a volley of arrows, severing her arms.  Lakshmana, equally enraged, cut off Tataka's ears and nose. The ogress, shapeshifting, briefly disappeared using her illusions, only to reappear and continue her rocky assault. Viswamitra urged Rama to end Tataka's reign of terror before nightfall, when ogres become more powerful. Heeding the sage's advice, Rama shot an arrow at the invisible Tataka, guided by the sound of her movements.  Despite her magic and illusions, she was trapped by Rama's arrows.  She charged at Rama and Lakshmana, roaring, but Rama swiftly shot an arrow into her chest, killing her.  The gods, witnessing this feat, rejoiced and praised Rama.  Indra, pleased, instructed Viswamitra to bestow upon Rama knowledge of powerful celestial weapons. The gods then departed, and as twilight approached, Viswamitra, relieved by Tataka's death, suggested they rest in the forest. Rama agreed, and they spent the night peacefully in the forest, now free from Tataka's menace. The forest, liberated from her evil presence, became as beautiful as the mythical Caitraratha grove.

__data__.text: Rama, expertly using the sound of Tataka's movements, pinned her down with a net of arrows, despite her illusions and magical abilities.  Enraged, she charged towards Rama and Lakshmana.  Rama swiftly shot an arrow into her chest, killing her. The gods rejoiced at Tataka's death, praising Rama for his feat. Indra, king of the gods, commanded Viswamitra to teach Rama powerful celestial weapons.  As twilight approached, Viswamitra suggested they rest in the forest, now free from Tataka's menace. Rama agreed, and they spent a peaceful night in the forest, which was transformed into a beautiful, safe haven, reminiscent of the mythical Caitraratha grove.

__data__.text: Vishwamitra, pleased with Rama, bestowed upon him divine missiles. He promised Rama victory over gods, demons, and other beings with these weapons.  Vishwamitra then listed the missiles, including Danda-Cakra, Dharma-Cakra, Kala-Cakra, Vishnu-Cakra, Indra-Cakra, Shiva's Sula, Brahmasira, Aisika, and a pair of maces named Modaki and Sikhari. He continued, granting Rama the noose of Dharma, Kala's noose, Varuna's noose, two Asanis (one dry, one moist), Pinaka, Narayana's missile, Agni's Sikhara, the wind-god's missile, Hayasiras, Kraufica, two Saktis, Kankala, Musala, Kapala, Kinkini, Nandana, Mohana, Praswapana, Prasamana, Varsana, Sosana, Santapana, Vilapana, Madana, Manava, the fiends' Mohana, Tamasa, Saumana, Sarnvarta, Mausala, Satya, Mayamaya, the sun-god's Tejahprabha, the moon-god's Sigira, Twasta's missile, Bhaga's missile, and Manu's Sitesu.  Vishwamitra then imparted the mantras to invoke these weapons to Rama and Lakshmana.  The missiles, personified, offered themselves to Rama's service.  Rama accepted them, commanding them to appear in his mind when needed.

__data__.text: Vishwamitra continued to bestow divine weapons upon Rama. He gave him Sakti, Kankala, Musala, Kapala, and Kinkini, weapons capable of destroying demons.  He then granted Rama the Nandana, a weapon of the Vidyadharas, and several weapons favored by the Gandharvas, including Mohana, Praswapana, Prasamana, Varsana, Sosana, Santapana, Vilapana, Madana, and Manava.  Vishwamitra further gifted Rama weapons of other beings: Mohana of the fiends, Tamasa and Saumana, Sarnvarta and Mausala, Satya, and Mayamaya.  He also granted Rama weapons associated with celestial bodies, including the sun god's Tejahprabha, the moon god's Sigira, Twasta's weapon, Bhaga's weapon, and Manu's Sitesu.  Vishwamitra then urged Rama to accept these powerful and versatile weapons.  Facing east, the purified sage happily shared the potent mantras for invoking these weapons with Rama.  These complex mantras were challenging even for gods to master. As Vishwamitra recited the mantras, the radiant forms of the weapons materialized before Rama.

The weapons, filled with joy, declared themselves Rama's servants, ready to obey his commands.  Rama accepted the weapons, instructing them to appear in his mind whenever he needed them.  Pleased, Rama then prepared to continue his journey with Vishwamitra.

These missiles, each with unique names and attributes, included Satyavan, Satyakirti, Dhrsta, Rabhasa, and many others with diverse forms and powers. Some resembled burning embers, others smoke, while some shone like the sun and moon.  Upon receiving them, the missiles, personified, offered their service to Rama, who instructed them to remain at his disposal, ready to assist when called upon in times of need.  The missiles then respectfully circumambulated Rama, agreed to his command, and departed.

Continuing his journey with Viswamitra, Rama observed a forested area that appeared like a dark cloud against a distant mountain. He expressed curiosity about this beautiful, deer-filled, bird-graced land, contrasting it with the unsettling forest they had left behind.  Rama then inquired about the area's details, eager to know whose hermitage it was and learn about the troubling presence of those who disrupted sacrificial rites. He reiterated his willingness to protect the area and eliminate the disruptive forces.

__data__.text: Viswamitra began to explain the history of Siddhagrama to Rama.  He recounted how Lord Vishnu, revered by all gods, resided in that very grove for countless years, practicing austerities and yoga. This grove, known as Siddhagrama, was once the hermitage of Lord Vamana, Vishnu's dwarf incarnation. During this time, the powerful demon king Bali, grandson of Prahlada, ruled after conquering the gods, including Indra and the wind-gods. Bali then commenced a grand sacrifice.  Concerned, the gods, led by Agni, approached Vishnu at Siddhagrama. They explained that Bali was granting boons to all who asked, and urged Vishnu to intervene for their sake before Bali completed his rituals.  Meanwhile, the radiant sage Kasyapa, accompanied by his wife Aditi, arrived.  After completing his own thousand-year penance, Kasyapa praised Vishnu, declaring that through his asceticism, he perceived the Lord as the embodiment of penance itself and the universe within his form.  He proclaimed Vishnu as his refuge.  Pleased, Vishnu urged Kasyapa to ask for a boon.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XXIX

Vigwamitra tells Sri Rama the history of Siddhagrama and arrives

__data__.text: Sage Kasyapa, through intense penance, perceived Vishnu as the embodiment of asceticism and the universe itself.  He sought refuge in Vishnu and was granted a boon.  Kasyapa requested Vishnu to be born as his and Aditi's son, a younger brother to Indra, to aid the gods against the demon king Bali.  Vishnu agreed, and the hermitage became known as Siddhagrama due to Vishnu's presence.  Vishnu, born as Vamana (the dwarf), reclaimed the three worlds from Bali and returned them to Indra.  Viswamitra explained that he resided in this sacred hermitage through his devotion to Vamana.  He urged Rama to slay the demons disturbing their rituals.  Finally, Viswamitra, declaring the hermitage to be Rama's as well, led Rama and Lakshmana into Siddhagrama, where they were greeted and honored by the resident sages.

__data__.text: Viswamitra led Rama and Lakshmana into the sacred hermitage of Siddhagrama, stating that it belonged to Rama as much as to himself, as Rama was an incarnation of Vishnu.  The sage, radiant with joy, entered the hermitage, resembling the moon emerging from behind clouds.  The resident sages, seeing Viswamitra's return with the two princes, rushed to greet and honor him. They paid their respects to Viswamitra and extended hospitality to Rama and Lakshmana.  After resting, Rama and Lakshmana respectfully addressed Viswamitra, urging him to commence his sacrificial rites and bless the hermitage so that its name, signifying success, would hold true.  They also expressed their hope that Viswamitra’s prediction about them slaying the demons would be fulfilled.  Viswamitra, a disciplined and devout sage, then began the consecration ceremony for the sacrifice.  Rama and Lakshmana, pure and composed, spent the night in the hermitage.  The next morning, they performed their morning rituals, including prayers and chanting, and then greeted Viswamitra, who was meditating after offering his prayers to the sacred fire.

Canto XXX

__data__.text: The princes, Rama and Lakshmana, inquired of Viswamitra the precise moment they should anticipate and defend against the ogres Maricha and Subahu. The assembled hermits praised the princes' eagerness for battle. They instructed the brothers to maintain a six-day and night vigil, explaining that Viswamitra had begun a period of consecrated silence.  Diligently, Rama and Lakshmana guarded the sacred grove, remaining sleepless and alert. Armed with their bows, they stood vigilant beside Viswamitra, protecting him and the ongoing sacrificial rites. As the crucial sixth day arrived, Rama urged Lakshmana to prepare for a conflict. As he spoke, the sacrificial fire blazed brightly.  The altar, adorned with sacred grasses, vessels, firewood, and flowers, became engulfed in flames as Viswamitra and the officiating priests presided over the ceremony.  Just then, a terrifying clamor erupted from the sky. Maricha and Subahu, along with their gruesome followers, descended upon the altar, cloaked in illusion and raining down a horrifying torrent of blood and gore. Witnessing the defilement of the altar, Rama sprang into action and spotted the ogres in the air.  As they charged towards him, he turned to Lakshmana and began to speak.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XXX

__data__.text: Maricha and Subahu, along with their monstrous followers, descended upon the sacrificial altar, cloaked in illusion and showering the area with blood and gore.  Seeing the defiled altar, Rama quickly identified the airborne ogres. As they rushed towards him, he addressed Lakshmana.  He declared his intent to scatter these vile, flesh-eating demons with the Manavastra, a divine missile.  However, he also stated his reluctance to kill them prematurely.  Despite this, he launched the powerful missile, striking Maricha and flinging him hundreds of miles into the ocean.  Rama then pointed out to Lakshmana the power of the Manavastra, which had incapacitated Maricha without ending his life.  Rama then vowed to eliminate the remaining demons who disrupted the sacrifices.  With impressive speed, he invoked the Agneyastra, a fiery missile, and killed Subahu.  He then used the Vayavastra, a wind-based missile, to banish the remaining demons, bringing relief to the sages.  Having vanquished the disruptive demons, Rama was honored by the sages, much like Indra after a victorious battle.  With the sacrifice completed and the area cleansed, Viswamitra expressed his gratitude to Rama, acknowledging the fulfillment of his request and the restoration of the sacred grove's sanctity.  He then proceeded with evening prayers alongside Rama and Lakshmana.

__data__.text: Rama, descendant of Raghu, eliminated all the demons disrupting the sacrifice. The sages honored him as Indra was honored after his victory over demons.  With the sacrificial rites concluded and the surrounding area cleared of threats, the great sage Viswamitra addressed Rama.  He expressed his gratitude, stating that Rama had fulfilled his request and that the sacred grove, Siddhasrama, had lived up to its name.  Viswamitra then praised Rama and proceeded with evening prayers, accompanied by Rama and Lakshmana.

__data__.text: Having successfully protected Viswamitra's sacrifice, Rama and Lakshmana spent the night at Siddhasrama. The next morning, after completing their morning rituals, they approached Viswamitra and the other sages.  They respectfully addressed Viswamitra, offering their services. The sages, with Viswamitra's consent, informed Rama about a sacred bow in Mithila, belonging to King Janaka. They invited Rama to accompany them to witness Janaka's sacrifice and to see the extraordinary bow, a divine gift originally bestowed upon King Devarata by the gods. This powerful bow had proven impossible for anyone, god or mortal, to bend.  The sages urged Rama to witness this marvel and Janaka's grand sacrifice.  They explained that the bow was given to Janaka as a reward for his sacrifice and is now enshrined in his palace.  Viswamitra then announced their departure, bidding farewell to the forest deities, and the group set off towards the Himalayas, located on the northern bank of the Ganges river.

Viswamitra declared their destination to be the Himalayas on the Ganges' northern bank.  A large caravan of carts carrying sacrificial supplies accompanied the traveling sages.  Even the local animals followed Viswamitra, but he kindly urged them to return. After a long journey, as the sun began to set, the sages paused their travels on the banks of the Sona river.  They performed their rituals and settled down for the night, led by Viswamitra.  Rama and Lakshmana respectfully greeted the sages and sat facing Viswamitra. Viswamitra, prompted by Rama's question, began to describe the region in detail to the assembled sages.

Canto XXXII

An account of the four sons of Kusa; the hundred daughters of KuSanabha
turn hunch-backed under a curse of the wind-god

__data__.text: King Kusa, a devout and righteous man born of Brahma, honored virtuous individuals. He had four noble sons through his wife from Vidarbha: Kusamba, KuSanabha, Asurtarajasa, and Vasu.  These sons were known for their brilliance, zeal, piety, and truthfulness. Kusa advised his sons to fulfill their Ksatriya duty of protecting the people, promising them immense merit in return.  Heeding their father's words, the four sons established cities. Kusamba founded Kausambi, while the pious KuSanabha built Mahodaya. Asurtarajasa founded Dharmaranya, and Vasu established Girivraja, also known as Vasumati, surrounded by five splendid hills. The Sona River, known as Magadhi, flowed through Magadha, enhancing the beauty of Vasu's city.  KuSanabha, a righteous royal sage, fathered one hundred exceptional daughters with the celestial nymph Ghrtaci. Upon reaching maturity, these beautiful daughters adorned themselves and enjoyed singing, dancing, and playing music in the royal gardens. Their beauty shone like stars amidst the clouds, captivating the omnipresent wind-god.

metadata.canto_summary:

An account of the four sons of Kusa; the hundred daughters of KuSanabha

Canto XXXII

An account of the four sons of Kusa; the hundred daughters of KuSanabha
turn hunch-backed under a curse of the wind-god

__data__.text: The hundred daughters of Kuśanābha, adorned in jewels and flowers, enjoyed the royal gardens, their beauty like stars amidst clouds.  The wind-god, captivated by their youth and grace, proposed marriage, promising them immortality.  The princesses scoffed at his advances, reminding him of his position and their own noble lineage. They declared their father would choose their husbands. Enraged by their rejection, the wind-god entered their bodies, contorting them.  Humiliated and weeping, the princesses returned to the palace, their deformities alarming their father, King Kuśanābha.  He questioned his daughters, desperate to understand the cause of their sudden, strange affliction.

__data__.text: The princesses, their bodies contorted by the wind-god, returned to their father's palace, filled with shame and distress.  King Kuśanābha, seeing his beloved daughters so drastically changed and miserable, was deeply troubled.  He questioned them, demanding to know the cause of their affliction and who had dared to violate the principles of justice.  He implored them to explain their strange silence and gestures, composing himself to hear their explanation.

Canto XXXIII

KuSanabha applauds the forbearance and forgiveness of his daughters;
the story of the birth of Brahmadatta and his marriage

__data__.text: Kusanabha's daughters explained to their father how the wind-god attempted to violate them. They had pleaded with him, explaining their obedience to their father's will and their willingness to marry him if their father approved. Despite their pleas, the wind-god, disregarding their virtue, harmed them.  King Kusanabha commended his daughters for their remarkable forbearance, a quality befitting women and men, especially towards divine beings.  He praised their unified stance, which protected their family's honor.  He extolled forbearance as the greatest virtue, equivalent to charity, truthfulness, sacrifice, and the foundation of the world.  After dismissing his daughters, Kusanabha, a powerful and wise king, consulted his advisors about finding suitable husbands for them.  Coincidentally, a radiant ascetic named Chuli began intense spiritual practices.  A Gandharva girl named Somada, daughter of Urmila, devotedly served him.  Pleased with her service, the sage offered to grant her a wish.

__data__.text: A Gandharva girl named Somada, daughter of Urmila, served the sage Chuli.  Pleased with her devotion, Chuli offered to fulfill her wish. Somada, unmarried and desiring a son, requested a spiritually enlightened one. The sage granted her wish, and a son named Brahmadatta was born. Brahmadatta became the king of Kampilya.  King Kusanabha decided to give all his hundred daughters in marriage to Brahmadatta.  The marriage took place, and as Brahmadatta took each daughter's hand, they were cured of their affliction.  King Kusanabha rejoiced at their recovery.  After the wedding, Brahmadatta departed with his brides and priests.  Somada, overjoyed at her son's marriage, embraced her daughters-in-law and praised Kusanabha before departing.

She embraced each of her new daughters-in-law and expressed her gratitude to Kusanabha before taking her leave.

__data__.text: Kusanabha, having no son, began a sacrifice to obtain one. During the ritual, his father, Kusa, descended from Brahmaloka and prophesied the birth of a pious son named Gadhi, who would bring Kusanabha lasting fame.  Kusa then returned to his celestial abode. In time, Gadhi was born to Kusanabha. The narrator, Viswamitra, reveals Gadhi was his father. Viswamitra also recounts the story of his sister, Satyavati, a devoted wife who ascended to heaven and became the Kausiki river, a source of blessing. Viswamitra lived by this river until his vow led him to Siddhasrama. He concludes his story, explaining his lineage and the history of the surrounding area, as requested by Rama.  Noting the late hour, Viswamitra urges Rama to sleep, as the forest is still and the night is deep.

__data__.text: Viswamitra explains to Rama how he arrived at Siddhasrama after leaving the Kausiki river due to his vow. He reiterates that he has now shared his lineage, the origins of his family, and the history of the region, as Rama had requested.  As the night is advanced, Viswamitra advises Rama to rest, emphasizing the need to avoid fatigue on their journey. He describes the tranquil forest scene: trees are still, animals and birds sleep, and darkness envelops everything. The evening twilight has faded, and the star-filled sky shines brightly.  The moon rises, casting its soothing light and bringing joy to all beings. Viswamitra warns that nocturnal creatures, including Yaksas, Rakshasas, and other predators, are now active.  After his narration, Viswamitra falls silent, and the accompanying sages praise him and the righteous lineage of Kusa, particularly Viswamitra's accomplishments and the sacred Kausiki river.  Impressed by Viswamitra’s story, Rama and Lakshmana also retire for the night.

Canto XXXV

Crossing the Sona, ViSwamitra and party reach the bank of the holy Ganga
and spend the night there. Requested by Sri Rama, he narrates
the story of the origin of the Ganga

__data__.text: Viswamitra, along with the accompanying sages, rested on the banks of the Sona river. As dawn broke, Viswamitra urged Rama to prepare for their onward journey.  Rama inquired about the best way to cross the Sona. Viswamitra indicated the ford used by other sages.  The group then crossed the river, and after traveling further, they reached the banks of the sacred Ganga river. The sight of the Ganga, frequented by swans and cranes, filled the sages, including Rama and Lakshmana, with joy.  They performed their rituals on the riverbank and settled down. Rama, filled with reverence, then asked Viswamitra to narrate the story of the Ganga's origin, a river which flows through the three worlds – heaven, earth, and the netherworld.  Viswamitra, prompted by Rama’s question, began to tell the story, starting with the Himalayas, the king of mountains and the father of Ganga, and his wife Mena, the daughter of Mount Meru.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XXXV

Crossing the Sona, ViSwamitra and party reach the bank of the holy Ganga
and spend the night there. Requested by Sri Rama, he narrates
the story of the origin of the Ganga

__data__.text: Viswamitra, prompted by Rama's desire to understand the Ganga's divine origins, began his narration. He described the Himalayas, the king of mountains, and his wife Mena, daughter of Mount Meru.  Their eldest daughter was Ganga.  The gods, recognizing Ganga's potential for divine purpose, requested her from Himavan.  He agreed, giving Ganga to them for the benefit of the three worlds. The gods departed, content with this sacred acquisition.  Viswamitra then spoke of Himavan's other daughter, Uma, who dedicated herself to a life of asceticism and devotion to Lord Shiva. He eventually gave Uma to Lord Rudra.  Thus, Ganga and Uma, both revered, became daughters of the mountain king.  Viswamitra explained how Ganga ascended to the heavens with the gods, initially becoming the celestial river Mandakini before descending to earth as the purifying Ganga.

__data__.text: Viswamitra concluded his tale of Ganga's origins, reiterating how the river ascended to the heavens with the gods. He explained that Ganga initially flowed as the celestial river Mandakini before eventually descending to earth in her purifying form.

Canto XXXVI

Gods interrupt the amorous pastimes of Lord Siva and His Consort, Goddess

__data__.text: Viswamitra finished telling the story of Himavan's daughters. Rama and Lakshmana praised the tale and asked him to elaborate on Ganga's story, her divine origins, and her journey through the three worlds.  Rama wondered why and how the purifying river came to flow through heaven, earth, and the intervening space, earning its reputation. He also inquired about Ganga's specific accomplishments in these realms. Viswamitra then began to narrate the following story to the assembled sages: Lord Shiva, recently married to Parvati, spent a hundred celestial years in conjugal bliss.  However, they had no children.  Concerned about the potential power of any offspring from this union, the gods, led by Brahma, approached Lord Shiva. They respectfully requested him to cease his amorous activities for the sake of the universe, suggesting he practice austerities with Parvati instead. The gods feared that the world wouldn't be able to withstand the glory of their offspring. Lord Shiva agreed, but questioned who would then bear the already released divine energy.  The gods suggested that Earth, capable of supporting all things, should receive it.

metadata.canto_summary:

Gods interrupt the amorous pastimes of Lord Siva and His Consort, Goddess
Uma, on which She curses gods, including Mother Earth

Canto XXXVI

Gods interrupt the amorous pastimes of Lord Siva and His Consort, Goddess
Uma, on which She curses gods, including Mother Earth

__data__.text: Shiva, agreeing to the gods' request, questioned who would bear the divine energy already released. The gods suggested Earth.  Upon receiving the energy, Earth became covered by it, prompting the gods to ask Agni, the fire god, to absorb it. Agni, aided by Vayu, concentrated the energy, forming a white mountain which transformed into reeds where Kartikeya, the fire-born, was born.  The gods, overjoyed, worshipped Shiva and Parvati.  However, Parvati, angered by the interruption of her conjugal bliss and desire for a child, cursed the gods with childlessness.  She also cursed Earth to have a varied surface and multiple rulers, and denied it the joy of having a son.  Witnessing the gods' distress, Shiva departed westward with Parvati to practice asceticism in the Himalayas.

metadata.canto_summary:

__data__.text: Parvati, angered by the interruption, cursed Earth, declaring it would never experience the joy of having a son.  Witnessing the gods' distress at Parvati's curse, Shiva departed westward with her towards the Himalayas. There, atop a peak named Himavatprabhava, Shiva and Parvati began their ascetic practices.  The narrator then addresses Rama and Lakshmana, indicating the conclusion of the story about Uma, daughter of Himavan (Parvati), and promises to tell the story of the Ganga river's origin.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XXXVII
A detailed account of Kartikeya’s birth through Ganga

__data__.text: The gods, led by Indra and Agni, approached Brahma seeking a commander for their army.  They explained that Lord Shiva, who had previously granted them a commander (in the form of seed), was now engaged in deep meditation with Parvati.  The gods implored Brahma to find a solution for the benefit of all worlds. Brahma reassured the gods, acknowledging Parvati's curse that prevented them from having children through their wives. He revealed that Ganga, the elder daughter of Himavan, would bear a son through Agni, carrying the seed of Shiva. This son would become the commander of the celestial armies and be accepted by both Ganga and Parvati.  Relieved, the gods venerated Brahma. They then traveled to Mount Kailasa and urged Agni to fulfill this divine purpose. They instructed him to deposit Shiva's seed, which he carried, into Ganga.  Agni agreed and approached Ganga with the request.  Upon hearing Agni's words, Ganga materialized in a radiant form.  The divine seed then permeated her being. Agni subsequently impregnated Ganga, ensuring that the seed filled her entirely.

metadata.canto_summary:

A detailed account of Kartikeya’s birth through Ganga

Canto XXXVII
A detailed account of Kartikeya’s birth through Ganga

__data__.text: Agni, carrying Shiva's divine seed, approached Ganga and requested her to bear Shiva's son, as per the gods' wishes. Ganga materialized in a radiant form, and the seed permeated her being. However, the intense energy of the seed caused Ganga great discomfort, and she released it onto the slopes of the Himalayas.  The seed transformed the surrounding area, turning earth into precious metals like gold and silver, and other materials into copper and iron.  The white reeds and mountain mentioned earlier also turned to gold, which from then on was called Jatarupa, meaning "born of form".  The gods and Indra then brought the newborn child to the Krttikas to be nursed. The Krttikas agreed that the child would be considered their son and nursed him. The gods proclaimed that he would be known throughout the three worlds as Kartikeya, son of the Krttikas. The Krttikas then bathed the radiant child.  The gods also named him Skanda, meaning "he who flowed," referencing his birth from Ganga.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XXXVII
A detailed account of Kartikeya’s birth through Ganga

__data__.text: The newborn, immediately upon birth, was nursed by all six of the Kṛttikās, who had agreed he would be their son. The gods declared that he would be known throughout the three worlds as Kārttikeya, son of the Kṛttikās.  After hearing this pronouncement, the Kṛttikās bathed the radiant child, who shone like fire.  The gods also named him Skanda, because he had “flowed” from Ganga.  Nourished by the six Kṛttikās, Skanda grew rapidly. Within a single day, despite his tender age, he gained the strength to conquer several divisions of the demon army.  The gods, led by Agni, then appointed the glorious child as the commander of their celestial forces.  This concludes the detailed account of the birth of Kārttikeya, a story that brings fortune and blessings.  Devotion to Kārttikeya promises long life and a place in his heavenly realm for the devotee, their children, and grandchildren.

metadata.canto_summary:

The narrative of King Sagara, a former king of Ayodhya
and a forefather of Sri Rama

Canto XXXVIII

The narrative of King Sagara, a former king of Ayodhya
and a forefather of Sri Rama

__data__.text: King Sagara, a pious ruler of Ayodhya, desired children but had none. His two wives, Kesini and Sumati, accompanied him to the Himalayas where he performed austerities for a hundred years. Pleased by his devotion, the sage Bhrigu granted Sagara a boon: one wife would bear a son who would continue his lineage, while the other would bear sixty thousand sons.  The queens inquired which of them would receive which boon. Bhrigu left the choice to them, explaining that one son would carry on the family line, while the many sons would be powerful and famous.  Kesini chose the single heir, while Sumati chose the sixty thousand sons.  The king and his wives then respectfully circumambulated the sage and returned to Ayodhya.

metadata.canto_summary:

Canto XXXVIII

The narrative of King Sagara, a former king of Ayodhya
and a forefather of Sri Rama

__data__.text: King Sagara presented his two wives with a choice: one would bear a single son who would carry on the lineage, while the other would have many powerful and famous sons. Kesini chose the single heir, while Sumati chose the sixty thousand. After thanking the sage, the royal couple returned to Ayodhya. In time, Kesini gave birth to a son named Asamanja. Sumati delivered a gourd-like fruit, from which sixty thousand sons emerged.  These sons were raised in jars of ghee and eventually grew to adulthood.  However, Asamanja, Sagara's eldest, cruelly threw infants into the Sarayu river and laughed as they drowned.  His wicked behavior, which included tormenting the pious and harming citizens, led to his exile.  Asamanja's son, Amsuman, was virtuous and well-loved.  After some time, King Sagara decided to perform a grand sacrifice and began preparations with his priests.

__data__.text: Sagara's eldest son routinely threw babies into the Sarayu river, laughing as they drowned. In contrast, Asamanja's son, Amsuman, was brave, kind, and beloved by all.  After some time, King Sagara decided to perform a grand sacrifice and began preparations with his priests.

__data__.text: King Sagara was performing a horse sacrifice, a grand ritual.  As the ceremony progressed, Indra, disguised as a demon, stole the sacrificial horse. The priests alerted Sagara to the theft, urging him to recover the horse lest the ritual be ruined.  Sagara then commanded his sixty thousand sons to search the entire earth for the horse and the thief, dividing the land amongst themselves for a thorough search. If they failed to find the horse above ground, they were ordered to dig into the earth itself until they located it.

__data__.text: King Sagara, assured by his priests that the horse sacrifice could still be completed, addressed his sixty thousand sons.  He instructed them to search the entire earth for the stolen horse. He assigned each son a square yojana of land to meticulously search. If the horse was not found on the surface, they were to dig into the earth until they found it. Sagara declared he would remain with his grandson and the priests until the horse was recovered. The princes, energized by their father's command, began their search, covering the entire world. Failing to find the horse above ground, they began to excavate the earth using their mighty arms, pikes, and ploughshares. The earth groaned under their assault, and the subterranean creatures, including nagas, demons, and other beings, roared in protest as they were unearthed and killed. The princes dug relentlessly, covering a vast area. Their excavation reached deep into the earth, disturbing even the beautiful realm of Rasatala. Distressed by the destruction, the gods, gandharvas, demons, and nagas approached Brahma, the creator, for help. They reported to Brahma the devastation being caused by Sagara’s sons, describing the widespread excavation and the killing of numerous creatures. Sagara's sons, convinced that someone had sabotaged their sacrifice, were destroying everything in their path.

They told him that Sagara’s sons were excavating the entire world, causing widespread destruction and killing many creatures, including those living in the water.  Driven by the belief that someone had stolen their sacrificial horse and disrupted their ritual, the princes were indiscriminately destroying everything and everyone they encountered.

__data__.text: The gods, frightened by the Sagara sons' destructive excavation, appealed to Brahma.  Brahma reassured them, explaining that the earth is Vishnu's consort and is currently supported by him in the form of Kapila. He foretold that the sons of Sagara would be destroyed by Kapila's wrath, a predestined event.  Relieved, the gods returned to their abodes.  Meanwhile, the excavation continued, producing a thunderous noise.  Sagara's sons, having searched the entire earth, reported back to their father, having found neither the horse nor the thief.  Sagara ordered them to continue digging.  Obeying their father, the sons dug down into Rasatala, where they encountered Virupaksa, a massive elephant holding up the earth. This enormous elephant supported the entire world, including its mountains and forests.  When Virupaksa shifted his head from exhaustion, earthquakes occurred.

__data__.text: Sagara's sixty thousand sons continued their excavation into the subterranean realm of Rasatala.  There they encountered Virupaksa, a colossal elephant resembling a mountain, supporting the earth on his head.  The sons respectfully circled the elephant before continuing to dig.  They then pierced through the eastern and southern quarters, encountering another massive elephant, Mahapadma, also bearing the earth's weight.  Again showing reverence, they circled this elephant and proceeded to the western quarter.  There they found Saumanasa, another giant elephant supporting the earth. After paying their respects, they moved on to the northern quarter where they met Bhadra, a snow-white elephant performing the same task. They touched Bhadra, circled him, and resumed digging.  Driven by their quest, they moved towards the northeast and continued their furious excavation.  Finally, they came upon the eternal Lord Vishnu, embodied as Sage Kapila, and saw the horse grazing nearby, filling them with immense joy.

__data__.text: Sagara's sons, driven by their fervent quest, dug towards the northeast.  There they discovered the venerable Sage Kapila, an incarnation of Lord Vishnu, and a horse grazing nearby.  Mistaking Kapila for the thief of their sacrificial horse, they angrily confronted him, brandishing their digging tools and rocks.  They accused him of the theft and threatened him, boasting of their lineage as Sagara's sons.  Kapila, enraged by their accusations and threats, responded with a dismissive sound. His divine wrath instantly incinerated the sons of Sagara, reducing them to ashes.

__data__.text: Amsuman, Sagara's grandson, was tasked with finding the missing sons and the sacrificial horse.  He was instructed to be respectful to those he met but defend himself against any threats.  Amsuman quickly began his journey through the underground passage dug by his uncles. He encountered a giant elephant worshipped by various beings.  After respectfully circling the elephant and inquiring about its well-being, Amsuman asked about his uncles and the horse.  The elephant assured him he would return with the horse.  Amsuman continued, questioning the other guardian elephants who offered similar assurances.  He finally reached the place where his uncles had been reduced to ashes. Distraught by their fate, Amsuman grieved deeply.  He then located the sacrificial horse nearby and wished to perform the water ceremony for his deceased uncles, but could not find any water.

__data__.text: Amsuman arrived at the place where his uncles had perished, reduced to ashes.  Overwhelmed with grief and sorrow, he wept for them. Nearby, he spotted the sacrificial horse.  He wanted to perform the customary water rites for his uncles but couldn't find any water. He then saw Garuda, the king of birds and his uncles' maternal uncle.  Garuda advised Amsuman that his uncles' deaths were divinely ordained and would pave the way for the sacred Ganga river to descend to Earth. He explained that Amsuman should not offer ordinary water but instead use the Ganga's water for the rites, as it would ensure the salvation of his uncles.  Garuda instructed Amsuman to return with the horse and complete his grandfather's sacrifice.  Amsuman obeyed, retrieving the horse and informing King Sagara of everything, including Garuda's message.  Sagara completed the sacrifice but remained perplexed about how to bring the Ganga to Earth.  Despite his long reign, he died without finding a solution.

__data__.text: Amsuman, Sagara's successor, ruled wisely and was followed by his equally reputable son, Dilipa.  Amsuman performed intense austerities on the Himalayas for a very long time, hoping to bring the sacred Ganga river to earth, but ultimately ascended to heaven without achieving his goal. Dilipa, grieving the loss of his grandfather and great-uncles, remained uncertain about how to bring the Ganga down to earth and perform the necessary rites for their souls.  While contemplating these matters, a righteous son, Bhagiratha, was born to him.  Dilipa, despite his glorious reign and numerous sacrifices, also passed away without resolving the issue of his ancestors' deliverance.  He appointed Bhagiratha as his successor and ascended to heaven.  Bhagiratha, a pious and childless king, entrusted his kingdom to his ministers and embarked on a prolonged period of arduous asceticism on Mount Gokarna, dedicating himself to bringing the Ganga to earth.  His unwavering devotion and austerities, lasting for a thousand years, eventually pleased the creator Brahma. Accompanied by celestial beings, Brahma appeared before the steadfast Bhagiratha.

__data__.text: Bhagiratha, a devoted king without an heir, entrusted his kingdom to his ministers and engaged in intense austerities on Mount Gokarna to bring the Ganga to earth for the salvation of his ancestors.  He maintained rigorous practices, including fasting and controlling his senses, for a thousand years.  Brahma, pleased with Bhagiratha's devotion, appeared before him accompanied by celestial beings.  Brahma granted Bhagiratha a boon, and the king requested that the Ganga descend to earth to cleanse the ashes of his ancestors and grant them passage to heaven.  He also requested a son to continue his lineage.  Brahma agreed, but warned that the earth could not withstand the Ganga's descent and advised Bhagiratha to seek Lord Shiva's help to bear the river's force. After instructing Ganga to assist Bhagiratha, Brahma returned to his abode with the gods.

__data__.text: After Brahma departed, Bhagiratha performed intense austerities, standing on his toe for a year to appease Lord Shiva.  Pleased with Bhagiratha's devotion, Shiva agreed to receive the Ganga, daughter of the mountain king Himavan, upon his head.  The Ganga descended from the heavens onto Shiva's head with great force.  Fearing the Ganga's arrogance and potential to flood the world, Shiva contained the river within his matted locks.  Unable to escape, the Ganga wandered within Shiva's hair for many years.  Witnessing this, Bhagiratha again engaged in deep meditation, further pleasing Shiva.  Then, Shiva released the Ganga into Bindusarovara.  The river divided into seven streams: three (Hladini, Pavani, and Nalini) flowed east, three (Sucaksu, Sita, and Sindhu) flowed west, and the seventh followed Bhagiratha's chariot as he journeyed onward. Thus, the Ganga descended from the heavens, to Shiva's head, and finally to the earth.

__data__.text: The Ganga, divided into seven streams, flowed in different directions. Hladini, Pavani, and Nalini flowed east, while Sucaksu, Sita, and Sindhu flowed west. The seventh stream followed Bhagiratha's chariot. As the Ganga flowed, it teemed with aquatic life. The gods, celestial beings, and sages witnessed this wondrous descent from the heavens to earth.  The water, considered sacred, cleansed those who touched it. Those cursed and banished to earth were purified and returned to their celestial homes.  Bhagiratha led the procession, followed by the Ganga and a multitude of beings – gods, celestial musicians, semi-divine beings, serpent deities, and aquatic creatures. The Ganga followed Bhagiratha's chariot, eventually reaching the sacrificial grounds of the sage Jahnu.  Jahnu, angered by the Ganga flooding his grounds, drank up its waters. Astonished, the gods and sages praised Jahnu and requested him to release the Ganga, making her his adopted daughter.

__data__.text: The Ganga descended to earth with a roar, teeming with fish, tortoises, and sea creatures. Its arrival captivated gods, celestial beings, and sages watching from their aerial vehicles and mounts. The water's brilliance illuminated the sky like countless suns. The churning water and foam resembled lightning and white clouds dotted with swans. The river flowed in various ways: swiftly, meanderingly, wide, narrow, turbulent, and calm.  The sacred water, having touched Shiva's head, cleansed all who bathed in it. Those cursed and banished from heaven were purified and returned to their celestial abodes. Bhagiratha led the procession, followed by the Ganga, gods, sages, celestial musicians, serpents, and aquatic creatures. The Ganga then flooded the sage Jahnu's sacrificial grounds.

However, pleased by Bhagiratha's request, he released the river from his ears, earning her the name Jahnavi, daughter of Jahnu. The Ganga then resumed following Bhagiratha's chariot, reaching the chasm dug by Sagara's sons and filling it.  The river descended into the underworld, Rasatala, to complete Bhagiratha's mission of liberating his ancestors.  Upon seeing his ancestors' ashes, Bhagiratha was overcome with emotion. The sacred Ganga washed over the ashes, purifying Sagara's sons, and their souls ascended to heaven.

__data__.text: Brahma addressed Bhagiratha after the Ganga's waters cleansed the ashes of Sagara's sons. He proclaimed that the sixty thousand sons were redeemed and ascended to heaven.  They would remain in heaven as long as the ocean existed on Earth. Brahma declared that Ganga would be known as Bhagiratha's daughter, and also as Tripathaga, Divya, and Bhagirathi, as it flowed through heaven, earth, and the underworld.  He instructed Bhagiratha to offer Ganga's water to his ancestors, fulfilling the vow made by his forefathers and himself.  Brahma emphasized that neither the glorious Sagara nor the virtuous Amsuman, despite their efforts and spiritual strength, could achieve this feat.  Even Bhagiratha's father, Dilipa, failed to bring Ganga to Earth.  But Bhagiratha succeeded, earning unparalleled glory.  This accomplishment brought him great merit.  Brahma advised Bhagiratha, though already pure, to bathe in the Ganga and reap the rewards of his virtuous deeds.  Finally, Brahma instructed him to offer water to his ancestors, blessed him, and departed for his own realm, suggesting Bhagiratha return to his capital.

__data__.text: Brahma commended Bhagiratha for fulfilling his vow and achieving supreme glory. He acknowledged Bhagiratha's remarkable feat of bringing Ganga to Earth, a deed that earned him immense spiritual merit.  Brahma then advised Bhagiratha to bathe in the Ganga and complete the ritual by offering its water to his ancestors. Following this, Brahma blessed Bhagiratha and returned to his celestial abode, suggesting Bhagiratha return to his kingdom.  Bhagiratha duly performed the rites for his ancestors, offering Ganga's water according to tradition.  He then returned to his capital, where his people rejoiced at his return, relieved of their anxieties. The narrator, Vishwamitra, concludes his telling of Ganga's descent to Rama, emphasizing the blessings associated with hearing and retelling this sacred story.  He notes that sharing this narrative brings satisfaction to one's ancestors and pleases the gods, while listening to it grants fulfillment of desires, eradicates sins, and increases lifespan and fame.

__data__.text: Viswamitra recounts a captivating tale, leaving Rama and Lakshmana in awe.  The brothers marvel at the story of Ganga's descent and the filling of Sagara's excavation, a night passing swiftly as they reflect upon the narrative. As dawn breaks, Rama expresses his gratitude and suggests crossing the Ganga. A conveniently placed boat, provided by pious Rishis, awaits them. Upon reaching the opposite bank and greeting the assembled Rishis, they spot the magnificent city of Visala. Viswamitra leads Rama and Lakshmana towards the city.  Rama, filled with curiosity, inquires about the city's ruling dynasty. Viswamitra, in response, begins to narrate an ancient legend concerning Visala's origins, emphasizing the story's authenticity and connection to Indra. He sets the scene in Satyayuga, a time of powerful and righteous sons born to Diti and Aditi.

__data__.text: Viswamitra continues his tale, explaining Rama's curiosity about Visala's origins. He assures Rama of the story's truth, detailing a time in Satyayuga when the sons of Diti and Aditi pondered achieving immortality. They devised a plan to churn the ocean of milk, using Vasuki as a rope and Mount Mandara as the churning rod. After a thousand years, the serpent, agitated by the churning, released a deadly poison called Halahala, threatening the universe. The gods pleaded with Lord Shiva for help.  Lord Vishnu appeared and, with a smile, offered the poison to Shiva as a mark of respect.  Shiva, accepting Vishnu's words, consumed the poison, saving everyone. The churning continued, but Mount Mandara sank into the depths.

__data__.text: After Vishnu offered the first product of the churning, the deadly Halahala poison, to Shiva, Vishnu disappeared.  Seeing the gods' fear, Shiva consumed the poison.  The gods and demons resumed churning.  Mount Mandara began to sink, prompting the gods to praise Vishnu and ask for help. Vishnu, taking the form of a tortoise, supported the mountain.  The churning continued, and after a thousand years, Dhanvantari, the embodiment of medicine, emerged, followed by radiant Apsaras.  These Apsaras, numbering six hundred million, were rejected by both gods and demons, becoming known as public women.  Varuni, daughter of Varuna and the deity of spirituous liquor, then emerged. The gods, sons of Aditi, accepted Varuni, while the demons, sons of Diti, refused her. This led to the gods being called Suras (those who accepted Sura or wine) and the demons Asuras (those who didn't).  The churning then produced the horse Uccaihsrava, the Kaustubha gem, and finally, the nectar of immortality.

The churning of the ocean continued to yield its treasures.  Varuni, the goddess of spirituous liquor and daughter of Varuna, emerged.  The gods, sons of Aditi, accepted her, while the demons, sons of Diti, refused.  This distinction led to the gods being called Suras (acceptors of Sura or wine) and the demons Asuras (those who refused it).  Following Varuni, the magnificent horse Uccaihsrava, the precious Kaustubha gem, and finally, the nectar of immortality emerged.

A fierce battle erupted between the gods and demons over the nectar.  The demons, allied with the ogres, fought against the gods, causing widespread chaos.  As the conflict threatened to destroy both sides, Vishnu intervened, disguising himself as a beautiful woman and taking the nectar.  Those who challenged Vishnu were defeated. The gods, sons of Aditi, ultimately triumphed over the demons, sons of Diti.  Indra, having secured victory and sovereignty, then ruled the three worlds, including the sages and celestial bards.

__data__.text: Diti, grieving the death of her sons, asked her husband, Kasyapa, for a son who could defeat Indra.  Kasyapa agreed, promising her a powerful son capable of killing Indra if she maintained ritual purity during a thousand years of ascetic practice. He blessed her and left.  Overjoyed, Diti began her arduous austerities at KuSaplava.  Indra, aware of her intentions, served her diligently, bringing her necessities like fire, grass, wood, water, and food. He also eased her fatigue with massages.  Nearly a thousand years later, Diti informed Indra of the impending birth of his half-brother, born of her austerities and intended to be his rival.  However, she assured Indra that she would reconcile them, and they would share dominion over the three worlds, which her son would conquer.  She reminded Indra that this son was granted to her as a boon by Kasyapa after a thousand years of asceticism.

__data__.text: Diti, feeling immensely pleased, informed Indra that her thousand-year ascetic practice was nearly complete, with only ten years remaining.  She told him he would soon have a half-brother, born of her austerities and intended to be his rival.  However, she assured Indra that she would ensure they were on friendly terms and would share dominion over the three worlds, which her son would conquer.  Diti reminded Indra that Kasyapa had granted her this son as a boon. As the sun reached its peak, Diti fell asleep, placing her feet where her head should have been.  Observing her impure posture, Indra entered her womb and used his power to divide the fetus into seven parts. The fetus cried out as Indra used his thunderbolt. Diti awoke and pleaded with Indra not to harm the fetus.  Respecting her plea, Indra emerged from her womb. He explained to Diti that he had divided the fetus because it was meant to kill him, asking for her forgiveness.

__data__.text: In the hermitage of Kuśaplava, Diti, distressed by Indra's actions, appealed to him. She requested that the fragmented parts of her fetus become the forty-nine wind gods, the Maruts,  inhabiting various celestial realms. Indra, accepting her proposal, declared they would be known as the Maruts. Subsequently, both Diti and Indra returned to heaven.  This sacred ground, once graced by Indra's presence, became the site of Visala, a city founded by King Visala, a descendant of Ikshvaku.  The lineage continued through Hemacandra, Sucandra, Dhūmrāśva, Srñjaya, the powerful Sahadeva, and finally, the righteous Kuśāśva.

__data__.text: Sage Viśvāmitra recounts to Rāma the history of the land they are in.  Diti and Indra, having resolved their conflict, departed for heaven from this very spot.  Ikshvaku's son, Visala, founded a city here named after himself.  Viśvāmitra details Visala's lineage: Hemacandra, Sucandra, Dhūmrāśva, Srñjaya, Sahadeva, and Kuśāśva.  Kuśāśva's son was Somadatta, whose son, Kakutstha, fathered the current ruler, Sumati.  Viśvāmitra suggests they rest for the night and meet Sumati in the morning. Upon hearing of Viśvāmitra's arrival, Sumati comes to greet him, offers him respect, and expresses his gratitude for the sage's visit.

__data__.text: Rama, upon seeing a deserted hermitage near Mithila, inquired about its history. Viswamitra explained that it had once belonged to the sage Gautama, who lived there with his wife Ahalya.  During Gautama's absence, Indra, disguised as the sage, approached Ahalya and seduced her.  Despite recognizing Indra, Ahalya yielded to his advances. After their encounter, Indra attempted to leave discreetly but encountered Gautama returning to the hermitage.  Seeing Indra disguised as himself, Gautama cursed him.

__data__.text: Upon discovering Gautama at the hermitage, Indra was immediately filled with fear. Gautama, radiating with spiritual energy from his recent ablutions and carrying sacrificial wood, appeared formidable.  He angrily confronted Indra, who stood disguised as the sage, about his deceitful act with Ahalya.  Gautama cursed Indra, causing his testicles to fall to the ground.  He then cursed Ahalya to spend thousands of years in the hermitage, unseen by all, subsisting on air and lying on ashes.  He decreed that she would only be redeemed from this state when Rama, son of Dasaratha, visited the grove. Upon receiving Rama’s hospitality, Ahalya would regain her original form and be cleansed of her transgression.  Having pronounced these curses, Gautama abandoned the hermitage and went to the Himalayas to continue his ascetic practices.

__data__.text: Upon arriving at the abandoned hermitage, Indra, deprived of his manhood, appealed to the gods. He explained his actions were for the benefit of the gods, preventing the powerful sage Gautama from claiming his throne.  As a consequence of the sage's curse, both Indra and Ahalya suffered. Indra pleaded with the gods to restore him, emphasizing his service to their cause.  The gods, led by Agni, consulted the celestial manes. They proposed a solution: transplanting the testicles of a ram onto Indra.  The manes agreed, promising those who offered similar sacrifices would receive blessings equivalent to offering a whole ram.  From then on, Indra bore the ram's testicles as a testament to Gautama's power.  Viswamitra then urged Rama to enter the hermitage and redeem the radiant Ahalya, who had endured hardship due to the curse.  Rama, with Lakshmana and Viswamitra leading, entered the hermitage and saw the celestial Ahalya, her form obscured and difficult to perceive due to Gautama's curse, yet shining with an otherworldly light.

__data__.text: Upon entering the hermitage, Rama, Lakshmana, and Viswamitra saw Ahalya.  Her radiance, intensified by her penance, was difficult to behold, even for celestial beings.  She appeared as a divine illusion, like a flame shrouded in smoke, or the moon veiled by clouds and snow, or a radiant halo surrounding the sun.  Ahalya, once imperceptible due to Gautama's curse, became visible with Rama's arrival.  Overjoyed, Rama and Lakshmana bowed at her feet, and she welcomed them, remembering Gautama's prophecy.  Composed, Ahalya offered them traditional hospitality, which Rama accepted.  A celestial celebration ensued, with flowers raining down and divine music filling the air. The gods praised Ahalya for her purity and obedience to Gautama.  Gautama, joyful and reunited with Ahalya, worshipped Rama and returned to his austerities.  Having received Gautama's blessings, Rama departed for Mithila.

__data__.text: Upon reaching Mithila, Rama, Lakshmana, and Viswamitra observed the impressive scale of King Janaka's sacrificial preparations.  Rama commented on the multitude of Brahmins and Rishis gathered from across the land.  He then requested Viswamitra to choose a suitable place for them to rest.  Viswamitra selected a secluded spot with water.  King Janaka, upon learning of their arrival,  immediately came forth with his priests to greet them. He humbly offered traditional hospitality to Viswamitra.  Viswamitra accepted the offerings and inquired about Janaka's well-being and the progress of the sacrifice. He also exchanged greetings with the accompanying Rishis and priests.  King Janaka then invited Viswamitra and the other ascetics to be seated.  Viswamitra accepted, and the king, along with his priests and ministers, sat around him according to their respective ranks.  Janaka then expressed his gratitude, declaring that the presence of Viswamitra and the ascetics had blessed his sacrificial preparations.

__data__.text: King Janaka, along with his priests and ministers, sat respectfully around Viswamitra.  Janaka expressed his gratitude for Viswamitra's presence, believing it blessed his sacrificial preparations.  He further remarked that the mere sight of the sage had brought him the fruits of the sacrifice.  Janaka informed Viswamitra that the sacrificial rites were to conclude in twelve days, after which Viswamitra would witness the gods arriving to receive their share of the offerings.  Janaka then inquired about Rama and Lakshmana, noting their god-like appearance, strength, and grace.  He asked Viswamitra about their lineage, the purpose of their journey on foot, and their impressive weaponry. He also expressed his curiosity about these two young men, who resembled each other closely and bore themselves with such dignity.  Viswamitra then revealed their identities as sons of King Dasaratha and recounted their courageous journey: their stay at Siddhashrama, their defeat of the demons, their visit to Vishala and Ahalya, their encounter with Sage Gautama, and finally, their arrival in Mithila to learn about the great bow. Having shared this information, Viswamitra concluded his narrative.

He further questions if his parents were reunited due to Rama's presence and if his father, Gautama, welcomed and honored Rama. Viswamitra assures Satananda that he dutifully performed his responsibilities, and Ahalya was reunited with Gautama.  Upon hearing this, Satananda welcomes Rama, praising him for arriving under the guidance of the invincible sage Viswamitra, whom he reveres as a powerful Brahmana Rsi and the ultimate refuge. He declares Rama blessed to have such a protector.

He extols Viswamitra's virtues, describing him as an invincible sage, a Brahmana Rsi of immense power and wisdom, achieved through rigorous asceticism.  Satananda considers Rama fortunate to be under such esteemed protection.  He then promises to narrate Viswamitra's might and true nature, recounting how Viswamitra, a pious and just king, ruled for thousands of years.  On one occasion, accompanied by a vast army, Viswamitra toured his kingdom, eventually arriving at the serene and abundant hermitage of Vasistha, teeming with diverse flora and fauna, and frequented by celestial beings, including gods, demons, Gandharvas, and Kinnaras.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, a powerful king, came upon the hermitage of Vasistha. The hermitage was a peaceful and wondrous place, inhabited by a variety of creatures, including deer and flocks of birds, and frequented by celestial beings such as gods, demons, Gandharvas, and Kinnaras.  It was also home to many Brahmana Rsis and celestial sages, including those who had attained great spiritual power through austerities.  These sages lived simple lives, some subsisting only on water, air, fallen leaves, or fruits and roots.  They had remarkable self-control, mastering both their external senses and their minds.  Among them were the Valakhilyas, dedicated to prayer and ritual offerings, and the Vaikhanasas.  To Viswamitra, the hermitage appeared to be a sacred realm, akin to Brahmaloka itself.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, a mighty warrior and devout man, humbly greeted the sage Vasistha. Vasistha welcomed him warmly and offered him a seat.  He then respectfully provided Viswamitra with fruits and roots.  Viswamitra inquired about Vasistha's well-being, his sacred rituals, students, and the surrounding plant life. Vasistha assured him that all was well.  After Viswamitra was comfortably seated, Vasistha inquired about his guest's welfare, asking if he governed righteously, cared for his subjects, and maintained his servants. He also asked about the status of Viswamitra's enemies, army, treasury, allies, sons, and grandsons.  Viswamitra replied that all was well in his kingdom and family. They conversed pleasantly for a considerable time, enjoying each other's company.  Vasistha then expressed his desire to extend hospitality to Viswamitra and his vast army, urging him to accept his offer of honor and respect.  Viswamitra graciously accepted, acknowledging that the very offer itself was a great honor.

Viswamitra graciously accepted, stating that the offer itself was a great honor, and that he felt well-received by the simple offerings of the hermitage and Vasistha's presence. He expressed his intention to depart after offering his salutations.  Despite Viswamitra's repeated attempts to take his leave, Vasistha insisted on his hospitality.  Viswamitra finally agreed. __data__.text: King Viswamitra and his army were generously hosted by Sage Vasistha.  Vasistha's miraculous cow, Sabala, provided a feast of abundant and varied delicacies, including sweets, wines, steaming rice, curds, and rich fruit juices.  Viswamitra, deeply impressed by the cow's bounty, offered Vasistha a hundred thousand other cows in exchange for Sabala.  Vasistha adamantly refused, declaring Sabala invaluable.  He explained that she was essential not only for his sustenance but also for his spiritual practices, including offerings to gods and ancestors, and the performance of sacred rituals.  Sabala, he affirmed, was his very lifeblood and source of joy.

__data__.text: Viswamitra insisted that Vasistha give him Sabala, claiming her as rightfully his.  Vasistha vehemently refused, declaring he wouldn't part with her for any amount of wealth—cows, silver, or gold.  He explained that Sabala was inseparable from him, like glory to a disciplined mind. She sustained his life, enabled his offerings to gods and ancestors, and fueled his sacred rituals.  She was the source of his ability to perform his spiritual practices, from daily oblations to grand sacrifices.  He reiterated that she was his everything, his wealth, his very lifeblood and joy.  Viswamitra, undeterred, increased his offer, promising fourteen thousand elephants adorned with gold, eight hundred golden chariots with white horses, eleven thousand fine steeds, and a vast herd of cows. Yet, Vasistha remained resolute, emphasizing that all his religious observances depended on Sabala.  He saw no need for further discussion, firmly stating he would not relinquish the cow that fulfilled all his desires.

Sabala, he insisted, was the source of all he needed and desired.

__data__.text: Viswamitra attempted to forcibly take the wish-yielding cow, Sabala, when Vasistha refused to give her up.  Distressed at being taken, Sabala lamented her apparent abandonment by Vasistha.  She questioned what she had done to deserve this treatment from the pious sage.  Sabala then broke free from Viswamitra's guards and returned to Vasistha, crying and asking why he had forsaken her. Vasistha reassured her that he was not abandoning her and explained that Viswamitra, empowered by his position and a formidable army, was too strong for him to directly oppose at that moment.  He pointed out the vastness of Viswamitra's army, filled with elephants, horses, and chariots.  Sabala, however, countered that a Brahmana's spiritual strength surpasses a Kshatriya's physical might, arguing that Vasistha's power was immeasurable and his glory unassailable, despite Viswamitra's strength.

__data__.text: Sabala, reassured by Vasistha's words, respectfully pointed out the superior strength of Brahmanas, derived from spiritual power, compared to the physical might of Kshatriyas like Viswamitra.  She declared Vasistha's strength immeasurable and offered to unleash her power to subdue Viswamitra.  At Vasistha's command, Sabala created an army of Pahlavas. These warriors began to decimate Viswamitra's forces.  Enraged, Viswamitra retaliated, destroying the Pahlavas.  Sabala then produced Sakas, a fierce warrior tribe resembling the Yavanas, who flooded the battlefield. These skilled fighters, armed with swords and spears, overwhelmed Viswamitra’s army.  Viswamitra, however, responded with powerful weapons, throwing the Yavanas, Kambojas, and Barbaras into disarray.

__data__.text: Viswamitra's forces, created by Sabala, were scattered by Vasistha.  From Sabala, more warriors emerged: Kambojas, Barbaras, Yavanas, Sakas, Mlecchas, Haritas, and Kiratas.  These new warriors swiftly defeated Viswamitra's army, including soldiers, elephants, horses, and chariots.  Viswamitra's hundred sons, enraged by their father's defeat, attacked Vasistha but were incinerated by his roar.  Witnessing this devastation, Viswamitra was overwhelmed with grief and shame.  Defeated and despondent, he instructed his surviving son to rule justly and then retreated to the Himalayan mountains. There he performed arduous austerities to gain the favor of Lord Shiva.  After a time, Lord Shiva appeared before Viswamitra and offered him a boon.

The inhabitants, including Vasistha's disciples and the animals, fled in fear. Vasistha, however, remained and reassured his disciples, vowing to defeat Viswamitra.  He then condemned Viswamitra for destroying his long-established hermitage.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, wielding a fiery missile, challenged Vasistha.  Vasistha, raising his staff like the rod of death, rebuked Viswamitra, a warrior, for daring to challenge him, a Brahmana, and vowed to crush both Viswamitra and his weapons. Vasistha declared his Brahmanical power superior to Viswamitra's warrior strength.  He neutralized Viswamitra's fiery missile with his staff, like water dousing flames.  Undeterred, Viswamitra unleashed a barrage of powerful missiles – those of Varuna, Rudra, Indra, Shiva, and many others, including weapons of sleep, confusion, and destruction.  Vasistha effortlessly neutralized each one with his staff, a testament to his superior Brahmanical power.

__data__.text:
Viswamitra continued his assault on Vasistha, launching a volley of potent missiles.  These included weapons associated with various deities and forces: Swayambhuva Manu, Gandharvas, sleep, yawning, inebriation, heat, cold, desiccation, destruction, Indra's thunderbolt, Brahma's noose, Kala's noose, Varuna's noose, Shiva's Pinaka missile, Indra's thunderbolts (both dry and wet), Yama's missile, weapons of fiends and mountains, the discuses of Dharma, Kala, and Vishnu, a missile of the wind-god, a churning missile, Hayagriva's missile, javelins, Kankala and Musala missiles, Vidyadhara's missile, Kala's missile, a trident missile, and Kapala and Kankana missiles.  Yet, Vasistha effortlessly neutralized every single one with his staff.

Frustrated, Viswamitra then resorted to the Brahmastra, a weapon of immense power.  Its deployment caused fear among the gods, celestial sages, Gandharvas, and Nagas, and alarmed the three worlds.  But even this ultimate weapon proved ineffective against Vasistha, who absorbed it completely.  As he did so, Vasistha's appearance transformed into a fierce, awe-inspiring form that stunned the three worlds.  Flames and smoke enveloped him, and his staff blazed like the fire of cosmic destruction.  Witnessing this, sages praised Vasistha's invincible power and urged him to quell his fiery aura. They celebrated Viswamitra's defeat and expressed relief for the world.

Accepting their praise, Vasistha calmed himself.  Viswamitra, humbled and defeated, acknowledged the superiority of Brahmanical power over Kshatriya might. Recognizing the futility of his weapons against Vasistha's staff, he resolved to embark on rigorous austerities to attain Brahmanhood himself.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, disheartened by his defeat against Vasistha, retreated to the south with his queen and began intense austerities.  He lived on fruits and roots, dedicating himself to this arduous practice.  Over a thousand years, he fathered four sons devoted to truth and righteousness: Havispanda, Madhuspanda, Drdhanetra, and Maharatha.  Brahma appeared and acknowledged Viswamitra's asceticism, declaring he had achieved the status of a royal sage. However, Viswamitra was ashamed and saddened, for he desired Brahmanhood, not just the rank of royal sage.  He resolved to continue his austerities. Meanwhile, King Trisanku, a truthful and disciplined man, wished to ascend bodily to heaven through a special sacrifice.  He consulted Vasistha, who deemed it impossible.  Undeterred, Trisanku traveled south to seek the help of Vasistha's sons, whom he found engaged in their own rigorous ascetic practices.

__data__.text: Brahma, accompanied by the gods, acknowledged Viswamitra's intense austerities, declaring him a royal sage before returning to his celestial abode.  Viswamitra, though honored, felt a deep disappointment. He had yearned for Brahmanhood, a higher spiritual rank.  Resolving to continue his arduous practices, he renewed his commitment to asceticism.  Meanwhile, King Trisanku, a righteous and truthful man, desired to ascend to heaven in his physical body through a unique sacrificial ritual.  When his request was denied by the sage Vasistha, Trisanku journeyed south to seek the assistance of Vasistha's sons, who were deeply immersed in their own ascetic disciplines.  Trisanku humbly approached them, acknowledging their spiritual authority and requesting their aid despite his prior rejection by their father. He pleaded with them to conduct the necessary rites for his celestial ascent, emphasizing his lack of any other recourse. He declared Vasistha's sons his only hope after being turned away by their father, their family's traditional priest and guide.

__data__.text: King Trisanku, rejected by Sage Vasistha and his sons, faced their wrath.  They questioned his audacity in seeking another priest after being dismissed by the truthful Vasistha, reminding him of the sage's unwavering support to the Ikshvaku dynasty. They declared their inability to perform the sacrifice Vasistha deemed impossible, advising Trisanku to return to his capital.  They emphasized Vasistha's power, capable of conducting sacrifices for all three worlds. Trisanku, however, persisted, stating his intention to find another priest despite their disapproval.  Infuriated by his defiance, Vasistha’s sons cursed him to become a Chandala, an outcast.  As night fell, Trisanku transformed. His skin and clothes turned black, his hair fell out, his garlands wilted, and his ornaments turned to iron.  Abandoned by his courtiers and citizens, the distressed king sought out the hermit Viswamitra. Moved by Trisanku’s plight, Viswamitra inquired about his purpose. Trisanku explained his failed attempts to perform the sacrifices for ascending to heaven bodily, his rejection by Vasistha and his sons, and the resulting curse. He lamented his misfortune, despite his righteous conduct and devotion. He expressed his belief in the supremacy of fate over personal effort.  He appealed to Viswamitra for help, considering himself a victim of cruel destiny.

__data__.text: With the night's passing, King Trisanku's curse transformed him.  His appearance became drastically altered: his skin darkened, his clothing turned black, his hair fell out, his garlands withered, and his ornaments turned to iron. Witnessing this, his courtiers and the citizens who had accompanied him abandoned him. Alone and distressed, Trisanku sought out the ascetic Viswamitra. The sage, witnessing the king's changed state and misfortune, felt compassion and inquired about the reason for his visit. Trisanku recounted his ordeal: his desire to ascend to heaven bodily, the failed sacrifices, the rejection by Vasistha and his sons, and the subsequent curse. He lamented his misfortune despite his righteous conduct, expressing his belief in the power of fate over human effort. Trisanku pleaded with Viswamitra for help, believing himself a victim of cruel destiny. He asserted that fate had thwarted his efforts and that he had no other recourse but to seek Viswamitra's aid. He declared his intention to seek no other refuge, emphasizing his complete reliance on the sage's intervention.

__data__.text: Viswamitra reassured the fallen king Trisanku, welcoming him and promising his protection.  He declared his intention to summon all the great sages to assist in a sacrifice that would allow Trisanku to ascend to heaven in his current form.  Viswamitra then instructed his sons to prepare for the sacrifice and sent messengers to gather the sages, including Vasistha's sons, emphasizing that any disrespectful remarks should be reported.  The messengers returned, informing Viswamitra that while most sages were coming, Vasistha and his sons refused, questioning how they could participate in a sacrifice for someone of Trisanku's degraded status.  Enraged by their insults, Viswamitra cursed Vasistha's sons to be reborn for seven hundred generations as outcasts, forced to live off the flesh of dogs and perform degrading tasks.  He further cursed Mahodaya to become a Nisada, doomed to a life of violence and cruelty.

__data__.text: Viswamitra's messengers returned, reporting that while many sages accepted the invitation to Trisanku's sacrifice, Vasistha, his sons, and Mahodaya refused.  They questioned the propriety of conducting such a ritual for a fallen king, expressing doubt that deities would accept offerings from such a ceremony and suggesting that any Brahmin partaking in the sacrificial meal would be defiled.  They further mocked Viswamitra's involvement. Upon hearing these disparaging remarks, Viswamitra, enraged, cursed Vasistha's sons to be reborn for seven hundred generations as outcasts, subsisting on the flesh of dogs and forced to live degrading lives.  He also cursed Mahodaya to become a Nisada, destined to a life of violence and without compassion.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, having used his yogic power to witness the downfall of Vasistha's sons and Mahodaya, addressed the assembled sages. He introduced Trisanku, a descendant of Ikswaku, known for his piety and generosity, who had sought Viswamitra's help to ascend to heaven in his current physical form. Viswamitra requested the sages to commence a sacrifice for this purpose.  The sages, recognizing Viswamitra's formidable power and fearing his wrath, agreed to proceed with the ritual, despite their reservations.  They appointed Viswamitra as the chief priest, with other knowledgeable priests assisting in their respective roles, performing the ceremonies according to prescribed procedures.  However, despite Viswamitra's prolonged invocation, none of the gods appeared to accept their share of the offerings, enraging the sage. He then raised his sacrificial ladle, ready to act on his own.
