__data__.text: Enraged, Viswamitra lifted his sacrificial ladle and declared to Trisanku that he would use his spiritual power to send him to heaven in his physical body.  He commanded Trisanku to ascend, claiming his austerities would ensure his success. As Trisanku rose towards heaven, Indra and the gods rejected him, citing a preceptor's curse and commanding him to return to earth.  Trisanku plummeted back to earth, crying out to Viswamitra for help.  Furious at this defiance, Viswamitra declared he would create a new heaven and new gods. He began by creating another set of constellations and a new lunar system in the southern sky.  Alarmed by Viswamitra's power and actions, the gods, demons, and sages pleaded with him, arguing that Trisanku, cursed by his teacher, did not deserve to ascend to heaven in his physical form. Viswamitra responded that he had given his word and would not break his promise to Trisanku.

__data__.text: Viswamitra's actions caused great concern among the gods, demons, and sages. They appealed to him, arguing that Trisanku, cursed by his preceptor, was unworthy of ascending to heaven in his physical body.  Viswamitra, however, stood firm, stating that he had made a promise and would not break it.  He declared that Trisanku would reside in heaven in bodily form and that the new constellations he had created would remain in the sky. The gods ultimately conceded to Viswamitra's demands. Trisanku was placed in the heavens, head downward, among the new constellations, which would orbit him.  The gods and sages then departed, leaving Viswamitra praised and his will accomplished.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, witnessing the departure of the sages from his hermitage, addressed them, suggesting they move to the western region near the Puskara lakes to continue their ascetic practices due to an impediment in the southern quarter.  He then proceeded to perform rigorous austerities, subsisting on roots and fruits.  Concurrently, King Ambarisa of Ayodhya commenced a horse-sacrifice.  However, the sacrificial animal disappeared, prompting the officiating priest to inform the king that a substitute, either the original animal or a human, was required to proceed with the ritual. King Ambarisa, accompanied by a large number of cows for trade, then embarked on a search for a replacement. His quest led him to Mount Bhrgutunga, where he encountered Sage Rcika, his wife, and sons.  After exchanging pleasantries, Ambarisa proposed to purchase one of Rcika’s sons for a hundred thousand cows to serve as a sacrificial offering, explaining his predicament and the urgency of the situation.

Rcika declined to sell his eldest son.  His wife then declared their youngest son equally precious and unavailable.  Sunahsepa, the middle son, upon hearing his parents’ pronouncements, offered himself to the king.  King Ambarisa, pleased with this turn of events, gave the sage a vast sum of gold, silver, jewels, and the promised cows in exchange for Sunahsepa, then promptly departed for his capital with the boy in his chariot.

__data__.text: King Ambarisa, accompanied by Sunahsepa, rested at Puskara.  While the king rested, Sunahsepa approached his maternal uncle, Viswamitra, who was meditating there with other sages.  Distressed and fatigued, Sunahsepa pleaded with Viswamitra for help, saying he had no family and begged the sage to protect him from his impending sacrifice. Sunahsepa wished for King Ambarisa's sacrifice to be successful but also for his own life to be spared, hoping to live long and perform austerities.  Viswamitra, moved by his nephew’s plea, asked his sons to take Sunahsepa's place as the sacrifice, believing it would benefit them in the afterlife. He reasoned that Sunahsepa, a helpless child of a hermit, deserved their compassion.  Viswamitra urged his sons to act virtuously and fulfill their duty, assuring them that this act would appease the gods and fulfill his own promise of protection to Sunahsepa.  However, Viswamitra’s sons, led by Madhucchanda, arrogantly rejected their father's request, questioning his decision to prioritize another's child over his own. They considered his proposal as inappropriate as consuming contaminated food.  This disrespectful response enraged Viswamitra.

__data__.text: At Puskara, Sunahsepa appealed to Viswamitra, highlighting the successful completion of Ambarisa's sacrifice and the fulfillment of Viswamitra's promise of protection.  Viswamitra's sons, led by Madhucchanda, arrogantly questioned their father's decision to save Sunahsepa, deeming it inappropriate.  Enraged, Viswamitra cursed his sons to live as outcasts for a thousand years.  He then instructed Sunahsepa to recite hymns to Indra and Vishnu during the sacrifice to secure his release. Sunahsepa, following Viswamitra's instructions, urged King Ambarisa to proceed with the sacrifice.  The king, joyful at Sunahsepa's compliance, quickly went to the sacrificial grounds.  Ambarisa then bound and prepared Sunahsepa for the sacrifice.  Bound to the sacrificial post, Sunahsepa sang hymns praising Indra and Vishnu.  Pleased with the hymns, Indra granted Sunahsepa a long life. King Ambarisa also successfully completed his sacrifice, receiving blessings due to Indra's favor.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, after a thousand years of austerities, was visited by the gods, led by Brahma, who bestowed upon him the title of Rishi.  Brahma returned to his celestial abode, and Viswamitra resumed his ascetic practices.  Later, the celestial nymph Menaka, sent by Indra, arrived at Puskara to bathe.  Viswamitra, captivated by her beauty, invited her to stay at his hermitage.  Overcome by infatuation, he spent ten years with her, unaware of the passage of time.  Eventually, Viswamitra realized that his involvement with Menaka was a divine scheme to disrupt his austerities, causing him deep remorse and regret. He felt ashamed and distressed at the lost time and the interruption to his spiritual pursuits.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, realizing that his ten-year relationship with Menaka was a divinely orchestrated plot to disrupt his spiritual progress, was filled with remorse and regret. He recognized that the gods had manipulated him into losing precious time and diverting him from his ascetic pursuits. Viswamitra then dismissed Menaka and journeyed to the Himalayas, determined to regain his spiritual focus.  He committed to lifelong celibacy and engaged in intense austerities on the banks of the Kausiki river for thousands of years, causing fear among the gods.  They appealed to Brahma, who acknowledged Viswamitra's efforts and offered him the title of Maharsi.  However, Viswamitra desired the title of Brahmarsi, a rank signifying complete control over his senses.  Brahma informed him he had not yet reached that level of mastery and encouraged him to continue his efforts. Viswamitra then resumed his even more arduous austerities, standing with arms raised, living on air, enduring extreme heat and cold, causing great concern among the gods, including Indra.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, after Brahma's departure, continued his rigorous austerities. He stood with his arms raised, surviving on air alone, without shelter.  He endured scorching summers surrounded by five fires, exposed himself to the open sky during monsoons, and stood in icy water throughout winters. He maintained this incredibly demanding practice for a thousand years.  His intense asceticism caused considerable anxiety among the gods, including Indra.

__data__.text: Indra, the king of the gods, tasked the celestial nymph Rambha with disrupting Viswamitra's intense meditation by enticing him. Rambha expressed her apprehension to Indra, fearing the sage's wrath.  Reassuring her, Indra promised to accompany her disguised as a cuckoo.  Rambha, adorned with captivating beauty, approached Viswamitra. The sage, hearing the cuckoo's song and noticing Rambha's presence, grew suspicious. Recognizing Indra's scheme, Viswamitra, enraged, cursed Rambha to become a stone statue for ten thousand years, prophesying her eventual release by a powerful Brahmana. Immediately, Rambha turned to stone, while Indra and Kamadeva, the god of love, fled.

__data__.text: Viswamitra declared that Rambha, for attempting to seduce him, a sage striving to conquer his base desires, would be turned to stone for ten thousand years. He then prophesied that a powerful Brahmana, possessing immense spiritual energy gained through austerities, would eventually free her.  Overcome with remorse for succumbing to anger and depleting the spiritual merit earned through his arduous penance, Viswamitra lamented his lack of control over his senses.  As a consequence of his curse, Rambha instantly turned into stone, while Indra and Kamadeva fled.  Viswamitra resolved to never again yield to anger or speak unnecessarily, even vowing to abstain from breathing and eating for centuries to regain control over his senses and attain the ultimate state of Brahmanhood. He pledged to continue his austerities until he reached this goal, declaring that his physical body would not deteriorate while he remained dedicated to his spiritual practice.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, leaving the Himalayas, traveled east and began intense austerities. He took a vow of silence for a thousand years, performing incredibly difficult and unparalleled ascetic practices.  He gave all his food to a Brahmin, remaining without any himself, yet upheld his vow of silence without complaint. Even after a thousand years of austerities and facing many obstacles, Viswamitra remained resolute and unyielding, his anger contained. When he finally broke his fast, Indra, disguised as a Brahmin, appeared and requested the food. Viswamitra, again, held his breath for another thousand years. Smoke emanated from his head, causing distress and confusion throughout the three worlds. Alarmed by his power and the disruption it caused, the gods, sages, and other celestial beings appealed to Brahma, concerned that Viswamitra's growing ascetic power, fueled by various temptations and provocations, posed a threat to the universe. They highlighted his flawless discipline and warned that if his desires remained unfulfilled, his austerities could destroy all creation. The cosmos reflected this turmoil, with oceans raging, mountains crumbling, earth quaking, and violent winds blowing.  The gods expressed their inability to find a solution and lamented the growing disbelief and confusion among the people of the three worlds.

__data__.text: The gods, deeply troubled by Viswamitra's escalating ascetic power, pleaded with Brahma. They described the widespread chaos caused by his austerities—turbulent oceans, crumbling mountains, a quaking earth, and violent winds.  Fearing that Viswamitra's unfulfilled desires would lead him to destroy the universe, they urged Brahma to intervene.  The sun appeared dimmed by Viswamitra's radiance.  The gods implored Brahma to appease the sage before he destroyed everything.  They conceded to grant Viswamitra any boon, even dominion over the gods themselves.  Brahma, along with the other gods, approached Viswamitra, hailing him as a Brahmarishi and expressing their satisfaction with his asceticism.  They granted him longevity.  Viswamitra, in turn, requested that the sacred syllable OM, the word 'Vasat,' and the Vedas come to him of their own accord. He also desired that Vasistha, a renowned sage and Brahma's son, acknowledge him as a Brahmarishi. Satisfied with the gods' promise, he bid them farewell.  Vasistha, urged by the gods, befriended Viswamitra, acknowledging his status as a Brahmarishi and confirming that all his desires had been fulfilled. The gods then returned to their celestial abodes. Viswamitra, having achieved the esteemed status of a Brahmana, honored Vasistha.

__data__.text: Viswamitra, having received the Brahmarishi title, was addressed by Brahma and the gods.  Viswamitra, pleased by their acknowledgement, expressed his remaining desires: possession of the sacred syllable OM, the word 'Vasat,' and the Vedas, and formal recognition from Vasistha.  The gods agreed, and Vasistha, compelled by the gods, accepted Viswamitra's elevated status.  The gods then returned to their heavenly dwellings. Viswamitra, having achieved his goal, honored Vasistha.  He continued his ascetic practices, traveling the world.  Satananda concluded his narration by praising Viswamitra's power and virtue. King Janaka, deeply impressed by the story, expressed gratitude to Viswamitra for attending the sacrifice. He acknowledged the benefits gained from hearing about Viswamitra's austerities. Janaka, noting the approaching evening rituals, requested to meet Viswamitra again the following morning and took his leave after respectfully circumambulating the sage. Viswamitra granted Janaka permission to depart.

__data__.text: King Janaka welcomed Sage Viswamitra, Rama, and Lakshmana.  Janaka offered his services to the sage. Viswamitra explained that Rama and Lakshmana wished to see the famed bow in Janaka's possession. Janaka agreed to show them the bow and began to narrate its history.  The bow belonged to the gods and was entrusted to his ancestor, Devarata.  He recounted how Lord Shiva, angered by the gods' refusal to share sacrificial offerings, threatened them with the bow.  The gods appeased Shiva, who then returned the bow.  Janaka further revealed that while preparing land for a sacrifice, he found a baby girl who emerged from the earth.  He named her Sita and raised her as his own daughter.

__data__.text: Viswamitra requested King Janaka to show the bow to Rama.  Janaka ordered his counselors to bring the celestial bow, adorned with sandalwood paste and flowers.  The counselors, possessing immense strength, retrieved the large, heavy bow from the city, requiring five thousand men to transport the chest containing it.  Presenting the iron chest to Janaka, the counselors remarked that this revered bow, worshipped by all kings, was now ready for Rama and Lakshmana's viewing. Janaka, addressing Viswamitra, Rama, and Lakshmana, explained the bow's history, stating that despite its worship by the Janaka dynasty and attempts by powerful kings, gods, demons, and other celestial beings, none had been able to string it. He then invited Viswamitra to show the bow to the princes.  Viswamitra instructed Rama to look at the bow.  Rama, opening the chest at Viswamitra's behest, declared his intention to not only hold and examine the bow but also to attempt stringing it.  Both the king and Viswamitra readily agreed, and Rama effortlessly grasped the bow.

__data__.text: At Viswamitra's urging, Rama looked at the bow.  Rama declared his intention to not just examine it, but also to string it.  The king and Viswamitra agreed.  Rama easily lifted the bow and, in full view of the crowd, strung it as if it were a child's toy.  As he bent the mighty bow, it snapped in two with a deafening roar that shook the earth and knocked everyone present, except for Viswamitra, King Janaka, Rama, and Lakshmana, to the ground.  When the crowd recovered, Janaka, relieved and impressed, praised Rama's incredible feat of strength to Viswamitra.  He declared that Sita would bring honor to their family by marrying Rama.  Janaka reaffirmed his vow that Sita could only be won through a feat of strength, and that she would now be given to Rama. He requested Viswamitra's permission to send his counselors to Ayodhya to invite Dasaratha to the wedding, instructing them to politely explain the events to the emperor and bring him back to Mithila. Viswamitra agreed, and the king dispatched his counselors with detailed instructions to recount the events and escort Dasaratha back.

They were to fully explain how Rama had won Sita's hand in marriage through his remarkable feat of strength.  The counselors were also to inform Dasaratha that Rama and Lakshmana had been under Viswamitra's care and protection.  Finally, they were to escort the delighted emperor back to Mithila for the wedding. Both Viswamitra and Janaka addressed the counselors, emphasizing the importance of accurately conveying the events and ensuring Dasaratha's return.

__data__.text: King Janaka's envoys, having rested for three days after their tiring journey, arrived in Ayodhya and entered the royal palace.  There they met the venerable King Dasaratha.  Reassured by the king's kind demeanor, the envoys delivered their message from Janaka.  They conveyed Janaka's polite greetings and inquiries about Dasaratha's well-being, as well as that of his advisors and priests.  Speaking with Janaka's and Viswamitra's approval, the envoys relayed Janaka's message.  They reminded Dasaratha of the challenge of the bow, and how Sita had been promised to whoever could string it.  They recounted how Rama not only strung the bow, but broke it, and thus won Sita's hand. Janaka, honoring his pledge, requested Dasaratha's permission to proceed with the marriage. He urged Dasaratha to come to Mithila with his advisors and witness the joy of his sons, Rama and Lakshmana.  The envoys concluded by emphasizing the kind and respectful nature of Janaka's message, which was endorsed by both Viswamitra and Satananda.  Upon hearing this news, King Dasaratha was overjoyed and addressed his own advisors, Vasistha and Vamadeva. He mentioned Rama and Lakshmana’s stay in Videha, under Viswamitra’s care.

The counselors and sages unanimously agree to the proposal.  Dasaratha, pleased with their agreement, decides that the journey will commence the following day.  Janaka's envoys, honored by Dasaratha's hospitality, happily spend the night in Ayodhya, content with the king's decision.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, after spending the night with his family and advisor, instructed his minister Sumanthra to make arrangements for the journey to Mithila.  He ordered the treasury officers to gather wealth and gemstones, guarded by soldiers, and to proceed ahead. The four divisions of the army – infantry, cavalry, chariots, and elephants – were to follow, along with well-equipped palanquins and other vehicles.  He requested respected Brahmins, including Vasistha, Vamadeva, Jabali, Kashyapa, Markandeya, and Katyayana, to travel ahead.  Dasaratha also ordered his own chariot to be prepared, emphasizing the need for haste due to the envoys' urging.  Following the king's command, the army marched behind him and the Brahmins. After a four-day journey, they reached the Videha territory.  King Janaka, upon hearing of Dasaratha's arrival, began preparations to welcome him.  Janaka greeted the elderly Dasaratha with joy and extended a warm welcome.  He expressed his delight at Dasaratha’s arrival, considering it a fortunate event.  Janaka mentioned the joy of Rama and Lakshmana, and the good fortune of Vasistha and other Brahmins accompanying Dasaratha. He declared that all obstacles to Sita’s marriage had been removed, expressing his happiness at the alliance with the Raghu dynasty. He urged Dasaratha to perform the wedding ceremony the next morning, after the completion of the sacrifice. Dasaratha listened attentively and acknowledged Janaka's words, reminding him that the fulfillment of a gift rests with the giver.

Rama and Lakshmana, led by Viswamitra, paid respects to their father, Dasaratha.  The king, overjoyed to see his sons, was further honored by Janaka's hospitality, and retired for the night in high spirits. Janaka, after completing the sacrificial rites and ensuring the well-being of his daughters Sita and Urmila, also retired for the night.

__data__.text: King Janaka, after completing his sacrificial rites, instructed his priest Satananda to summon his brother, Kuśadhvaja, from Śaṅkāśyā.  Kuśadhvaja, described as virtuous and valiant, resided in the prosperous city, enjoying the waters of the Ikṣumati River. Janaka desired his brother's presence at the upcoming wedding. Messengers were dispatched and, upon receiving the message, Kuśadhvaja promptly traveled to Mithila.  He greeted Janaka and Satananda respectfully before being seated. The brothers then sent their counselor, Sudamana, to invite King Daśaratha and his entourage to Mithila. Sudamana conveyed the invitation, and Daśaratha, along with his sons, counselors, and sages, journeyed to Janaka's court. There, Daśaratha requested Sage Vasiṣṭha to recount his lineage.  Vasiṣṭha began by describing the origin of Brahma and traced the line through Marīci, Kaśyapa, Vivasvat, Manu, and Ikṣvāku, the first king of Ayodhyā, continuing through  Kuksi, Vikuksi, Bāṇa, Anaraṇya, Dhundhumāra, Yuvanāśva, Māndhātā, Susandhi, Dhruvasandhi, and Prasenajit.

__data__.text: King Daśaratha arrived at Janaka's court in Mithila with his sons, advisors, and sages.  Janaka, seated with his brother Kuśadhvaja, welcomed them.  Daśaratha then requested Sage Vasiṣṭha to recount his lineage to Janaka. Vasiṣṭha began by describing the creation of Brahma and traced the lineage through prominent figures like Marīci, Kaśyapa, Vivasvat, Manu, and finally to Ikṣvāku, the first king of Ayodhyā.  He continued the lineage through Kuksi, Vikuksi, Bāṇa, Anaraṇya,  and  Dhundhumāra. Vasiṣṭha then told the story of King Asita, who, after being defeated in battle, fled to the Himalayas with his pregnant wives and subsequently died.  One of the wives attempted to poison the other's unborn child.  A sage named Cyavana then encountered the poisoned queen, Kalindi, and prophesied that she would bear a powerful son, who would be named Sagara because he was born with the poison. Vasiṣṭha then continued the lineage from Sagara to Asamañja, Amśumān, Dilīpa and finally to Bhagiratha.

__data__.text: Vasiṣṭha continued recounting the Ikshvaku lineage. He spoke of Dhundhumāra, Yuvanāśva, Māndhātā, Susandhi, Dhruvasandhi, and Bharata.  Then came Asita, who was defeated by his enemies and fled to the Himalayas with his pregnant wives, where he died.  One wife attempted to harm the other's unborn child with poison.  The sage Cyavana encountered the poisoned queen, Kalindi, and foretold the birth of her powerful son.  Vasiṣṭha then swiftly moved the narrative through the generations:  Agnivarna, Sighraga, Maru, Prasusruka, Ambarisa, Nahusa, Yayati, Nabhaga, Aja, and finally to Daśaratha, father of Rama and Lakshmana. Vasiṣṭha concluded by formally requesting Janaka's daughters for Rama and Lakshmana, emphasizing their noble lineage and virtuous qualities.

__data__.text: Sage Vasistha continued his recounting of the Ikshvaku lineage.  A widowed princess, devoted to her deceased husband, gave birth to a son, Sagara, who was miraculously unharmed by poison meant for him in the womb.  Sagara's descendants included Asamañja, Amśumān, Dilīpa, and Bhagiratha.  From Bhagiratha came Kakutstha, and then Raghu, whose son Pravṛddha became known as Kalmāṣapāda due to a curse.  The lineage continued through Śaṅkhana, Sudarśana, Agnivarna, Sighraga, Maru, Prasusruka, Ambarisa, Nahusa, Yayati, and Nābhaga.  Finally, Vasiṣṭha arrived at Aja, father of Daśaratha, who in turn was the father of Rama and Lakshmana.  Vasiṣṭha formally requested Janaka to grant his daughters in marriage to Rama and Lakshmana, highlighting their noble lineage, virtuous character, and descent from the ancient and pure Ikshvaku line.

__data__.text: King Janaka, addressing Sage Vasistha, began to recount his lineage. He explained the importance of sharing one's ancestry, especially when giving a daughter in marriage.  He then detailed his family history, starting with King Nimi, renowned for his piety and deeds. The lineage continued through Mithi, the first king named Janaka, and subsequent rulers like Udavasu, Nandivardhana, Suketu, Devarata, Brhadratha, Mahavira, Sudhrti, Dhrstaketu, Haryaswa, Maru, Pratindhaka, and Kirtiratha.  The line continued through Devamidha, Vibudha, Mahidhraka, another Kirtirata, Maharoma, Swarnaroma, and Hraswaroma.  Janaka concluded by stating that Hraswaroma had two sons: himself, the elder, and his valiant brother, KuSadhwaja.  Their father, upon crowning Janaka king, entrusted KuSadhwaja to his care before retiring to the forest.  After their father's passing, Janaka diligently ruled the kingdom while affectionately caring for his brother.

__data__.text: King Janaka continued his narration to Sage Vasistha. He told of his father, Hraswaroma, born to Swarnaroma, who in turn was the son of Maharoma.  Janaka explained that he was the elder of two sons, his younger brother being the valiant KuSadhwaja.  Upon their father's retirement, Janaka inherited the throne and became KuSadhwaja's guardian. After their father's passing, Janaka ruled righteously while caring for his brother.  Sometime later, King Sudhanva of Sankasya besieged Mithila, demanding Sita's hand in marriage and Lord Shiva's bow. Janaka refused, leading to a battle where he defeated and killed Sudhanva. Following this, Janaka installed his brother KuSadhwaja as the ruler of Sankasya.  Finally, Janaka joyfully offered his daughters Sita and Urmila to Rama and Lakshmana respectively. He then advised King Dasaratha to conduct the necessary ceremonies for the weddings, including the *Samavartana and Nandisraddha rituals, suggesting the auspicious day of Uttara Phalguni for the marriage itself.

__data__.text: King Janaka, addressing King Dasaratha, declared that the current day was auspicious due to the ascendance of the Magha constellation.  He advised Dasaratha to conduct the wedding ceremony three days later when the Uttara Phalguni constellation would be ascendant.  Janaka further recommended that gifts be presented to Rama and Lakshmana for their well-being and future happiness.

__data__.text: Sages Viśvāmitra and Vasiṣṭha addressed King Janaka, praising the lineages of both the Ikṣvāku and Videha families.  They proposed a marriage alliance, deeming Sītā and Ūrmilā suitable matches for Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa, respectively.  Further, they suggested that King Kusadhvaja's daughters be wed to Bharata and Śatrughna.  They extolled the virtues and prowess of Daśaratha's four sons.  King Janaka, honored by the proposal, readily agreed, suggesting the second day of Phālguni as an auspicious date for the weddings. He humbly offered seats to the sages, acknowledging his servitude to them.

__data__.text: King Daśaratha finalized the marriage arrangements for his four sons.  The sages suggested the second day of Phālguni as an auspicious wedding date. King Janaka humbly accepted the proposal, expressing his deference to the sages and offering them honored seats.  He affirmed that Mithilā was as much Daśaratha's as Ayodhyā was his own.  Daśaratha graciously acknowledged Janaka's hospitality, mentioning the many honored guests the king had received.  He then announced his departure to perform the necessary Śrāddha rites. After taking leave of Janaka, Daśaratha returned to his lodging with the sages. The following morning, he performed the Samāvartana ceremony for his sons, which involved shaving their heads and armpits and gifting cows.  As part of this ceremony, the generous king donated a hundred thousand cows per son to the Brahmins, totaling four hundred thousand cows, along with other riches.  Surrounded by his sons, Daśaratha radiated like Brahmha amidst the guardians of the universe.

__data__.text: King Daśaratha, deeply devoted to his sons, generously gifted four hundred thousand recently calved cows to the Brahmins as part of the Samāvartana ceremony. These cows, adorned with gold-plated horns and accompanied by bell-metal milking vessels, were known for their abundant milk yield.  He also bestowed a vast amount of additional wealth upon the Brahmins.  Surrounded by his sons, who had completed the  ceremony, the radiant king resembled the benevolent creator god Brahmā amidst the celestial guardians.

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available

__data__.text:
Canto LXXIII: The Wedding Preparations

As King Dasaratha performed the grand *Samavartana* ceremony, Yudhajit, the king of Kekeya and Bharata's maternal uncle, arrived in Mithila. After exchanging pleasantries, Yudhajit conveyed his father's well wishes and explained his own journey.  He had initially travelled to Ayodhya intending to see Bharata, but upon learning of the wedding party's departure for Mithila, he hastened there to witness the event.  King Dasaratha warmly welcomed his guest. The following morning, after completing his rituals, Dasaratha led the sages to the wedding pavilion.  Rama, adorned in festive attire and accompanied by his brothers, also proceeded to the pavilion at the auspicious hour of *Vijaya*.  Vasistha then addressed Janaka, informing him of Dasaratha's presence with his sons and requesting him to proceed with the wedding ceremony, highlighting the importance of both the giver and receiver in completing the sacred rite.  Janaka graciously responded, expressing his shared ownership of the kingdom with Dasaratha and welcoming him without reservation. He informed Vasistha that his daughters, radiant as flames, were already waiting at the altar.

Narada, a celestial sage, is questioned by the ascetic Valmiki about the existence of a perfect man. Valmiki inquires if there is anyone in the world currently who embodies a multitude of virtues:  strength, righteousness, gratitude, truthfulness, resoluteness, proper conduct, and kindness towards all beings. He further asks if there is anyone with knowledge, power, a captivating presence, self-control, a calm temper, brilliance, and a flawless character, whose wrath is feared even by the gods. Valmiki expresses his eagerness to learn about such an individual, believing Narada possesses the wisdom to identify such a person.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Narada, acknowledging Valmiki's query, agrees to tell him about a man possessing the virtues the sage described.  He reveals that this man, named Rama, is a descendant of Ikswaku. Rama is described as self-controlled, powerful, radiant, resolute, and disciplined.  He is also intelligent, wise, eloquent, glorious, and a vanquisher of enemies.  Physically, Rama is depicted as having broad shoulders, strong arms, a conch-shaped neck, and a strong chin.  He also possesses a broad chest, wields a mighty bow, and has a well-developed physique. His long arms reach his knees, and he has a well-proportioned head, a fine forehead, and an elegant gait.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama is described as having a balanced physique, neither exceptionally tall nor short, with well-proportioned limbs and a radiant complexion. His powerful presence is complemented by a full chest and expressive eyes, further enhanced by fortunate markings on his body.  Known for his unwavering commitment to virtue and truthfulness, Rama dedicates himself to the well-being of his people. His wisdom and purity shine through in all his actions, and his remarkable self-control allows him to maintain a focused mind. As a benevolent leader, Rama sustains creation, much like Brahma, the creator god. He possesses great wealth and fearlessly defeats his enemies, protecting all living beings and upholding righteousness. He safeguards not only his own principles but also the welfare of his people.  A master of ancient knowledge, Rama understands the profound truths of the Vedas and their associated sciences. His expertise in archery is unmatched.  His deep understanding extends to all scriptures, and his sharp memory and quick wit enable him to grasp complex concepts effortlessly. Beloved across all realms, Rama embodies piety and possesses a noble spirit, coupled with astute judgment.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama is consistently sought after by righteous individuals, just as rivers naturally flow towards the ocean.  He possesses a noble character, treating everyone equally with a pleasant and welcoming demeanor.  He embodies all positive qualities, bringing joy to his mother, Kausalya.  His profound nature is comparable to the vastness of the ocean, while his unwavering resolve mirrors the steadfastness of the Himalayas.  In strength and power, Rama is likened to Lord Vishnu, and his appearance is as soothing and pleasing as the moon.  While his anger can be as fierce as the destructive fire at the end of time, his capacity for forgiveness is as boundless as the Earth itself.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: King Dasharatha wished to appoint his eldest son, Rama, as regent. Rama was known for his generosity, truthfulness, and commitment to the welfare of the people.  However, Queen Kaikeyi, using a previously granted boon, requested that her son, Bharata, be made regent instead, and that Rama be exiled.  Bound by his word, King Dasharatha sorrowfully agreed to exile Rama.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama, upholding his father's promise to Kaikeyi, dutifully accepted his exile to the forest.  He was followed by his devoted younger brother, Lakshmana, who was known for his respectful nature and brought joy to his mother, Sumitra.  Rama's beloved wife, Sita, also accompanied him.  She was considered a blessing, possessing all the noble qualities and was as dear to Rama as life itself.  The people of Ayodhya, along with King Dasharatha, followed Rama for some distance, demonstrating their love and loyalty.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita, met Guha, the chief of the Nisadas, at Srngaverapura.  They then journeyed through forests and across rivers, eventually reaching Chitrakoota on the advice of Sage Bharadwaja. There, they built a comfortable dwelling and lived happily.  Meanwhile, back in Ayodhya, King Dasharatha, heartbroken over Rama's exile, passed away.  Bharata, despite being urged to take the throne, refused and instead journeyed to the forest to convince Rama to return and rule.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Bharata pleaded with Rama to return to Ayodhya and rule. He insisted that Rama was the rightful king. However, Rama, honoring his father's decree, declined the kingship.  Instead, he gave Bharata his sandals, symbolizing his authority, and urged him to return and govern Ayodhya.  Disappointed but respectful of Rama's decision, Bharata took the sandals and departed, touching Rama's feet. After Bharata's departure, Rama, determined to fulfill his exile, anticipated further visits from Ayodhya residents and moved deeper into the Dandaka forest.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: After entering the Dandaka forest and defeating the ogre Viradha, Rama encountered several sages, including Sarabhanga, Sutiksna, Agastya, and Agastya's brother.  Agastya gifted Rama a divine bow, sword, and quivers with an endless supply of arrows, originally given to Agastya by Indra. As Rama continued his stay in the forest, he was approached by a group of sages who lived there. They asked him to protect them from the demons and ogres that plagued the forest.  Before these sages, Rama vowed to eliminate these troublesome creatures.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: While Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana resided in the Dandaka forest, they encountered Surpanakha, a shape-shifting ogress who lived in a part of the forest known as Janasthana.  Surpanakha was known for her unusually large nails.  It was in this forest that she was disfigured by Lakshmana, who cut off her nose and ears.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Ravana, enraged by the news of his kinsmen's deaths, enlisted the help of the ogre Marica. Despite Marica's warnings against provoking Rama, Ravana, driven by his own demise, proceeded to Rama's hermitage.  Marica lured Rama and Lakshmana away, allowing Ravana to abduct Sita after mortally wounding Jatayu, who tried to intervene. Grief-stricken by Jatayu's injury and Sita's abduction, Rama cremated the noble bird. While searching for Sita, he encountered the grotesque ogre Kabandha.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama cremated Kabandha, who, upon death, regained his original Gandharva form and ascended to heaven.  Before departing, Kabandha advised Rama to seek out Sabari, a righteous hermitess. Following this advice, Rama found Sabari and was duly honored by her.  Subsequently, on the banks of the Pampa lake, Rama met the monkey chief, Hanuman.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman facilitated a meeting between Rama and Sugriva.  Rama recounted his life story, including Sita's abduction, to Sugriva.  Impressed by Rama's tale, Sugriva formed a pact of friendship with him, symbolized by a sacred fire.  Sugriva then explained the source of his conflict with his brother, Vali, to a sympathetic Rama.  Rama, in turn, vowed to kill Vali.  Sugriva, however, remained apprehensive about Rama's ability to defeat Vali and described his brother's immense strength.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Sugriva, to demonstrate Vali's immense strength, showed Rama the colossal skeleton of the demon Dundubhi, which resembled a large mountain and had been effortlessly kicked a great distance by Vali.  Rama, unfazed, flicked the massive skeleton an even greater distance with his toe.  To further reassure Sugriva, Rama then pierced seven palmyra trees, a nearby hill, and even the subterranean realms with a single arrow.  Sugriva, now confident in Rama's power, joyfully returned to Kiskindha with him.  Sugriva's triumphant roar upon their arrival prompted Vali to emerge from the city.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama, after reassuring Tara, Vali's wife, who warned against fighting Sugriva now allied with Rama, killed Vali with a single arrow.  Following this, Rama instated Sugriva as the new king. Sugriva then assembled all the monkeys and sent them out to search for Sita.  Acting on the advice of Sampati, Hanuman leaped across the vast ocean separating India from Lanka.  He then located Sita in an Asoka grove in Ravana's city of Lanka.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Hanuman, after presenting Rama's ring to Sita and reassuring her of Rama's imminent arrival to rescue her from Ravana, proceeded to cause significant damage to Lanka's defenses. He defeated several of Lanka's military leaders, including five army commanders and seven sons of ministers, as well as a prominent warrior named Aksa.  Although briefly captured when Indrajit bound him with a Brahmastra, Hanuman strategically allowed himself to be taken to Ravana.  Following his audience with Ravana, Hanuman set fire to a large portion of Lanka, intentionally sparing Sita's quarters. He then returned to Rama to report Sita's location and well-being.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Reaching the southern shore with Sugriva, Rama demonstrated his power by disturbing the ocean with his radiant arrows when it initially refused to provide passage for his army to Lanka. The ocean then appeared before Rama, apologizing for its resistance.  At the ocean's suggestion, Rama had Nala construct a bridge across the sea. Crossing the bridge, Rama defeated Ravana in battle and recovered Sita. However, Rama felt deeply troubled about Sita's extended stay in Ravana's custody. He expressed his feelings to Sita in front of the assembled monkeys and others, causing Sita great distress, leading her to enter a fire to prove her purity.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: After the fire god Agni testified to Sita's purity, Rama accepted her back.  This act brought joy to the three worlds, including gods and sages, who celebrated Rama's victory over Ravana and Sita's rescue.  Rama, honored by the gods, was filled with happiness.  He then installed Vibhishana as the new king of Lanka, feeling a sense of accomplishment and relief.  The gods granted Rama a boon to revive the fallen monkey warriors.  With Sita, Lakshmana, Vibhishana, Sugriva, and other friends, Rama departed for Ayodhya in Ravana's celestial chariot, Pushpaka.  On their journey, they stopped at the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja, where Rama sent Hanuman ahead to inform Bharata of his return.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama, along with Sugriva and others, recounted the events leading to his exile as they traveled in the Pushpaka chariot to Nandigrama, a part of Ayodhya.  At Nandigrama, Rama, reunited with his brothers and Sita,  untangled his matted hair, symbolizing the end of his exile and the reclaiming of his kingdom.  His reign promised prosperity and well-being for all: happiness, contentment, piety, freedom from illness and worry, and an end to famine and fear.  No parent would suffer the loss of a child, no woman would become a widow, and everyone would live without fear of fire, flood, windstorms, or disease.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: During Rama's reign, there will be no hunger or theft. Cities and states will overflow with wealth and food.  Everyone will experience the joy and prosperity reminiscent of the Satyayuga (the golden age).  Rama will perform numerous horse sacrifices and other ceremonies involving vast amounts of gold. He will generously donate countless cows to scholars and immense riches to Brahmins.  He will establish and support numerous prosperous royal dynasties.  Furthermore, he will guide people of all social classes to fulfill their respective duties.  After ruling for eleven thousand years, Rama will ascend to Brahmaloka.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Reading the sacred narrative of Rama, comparable to the Vedas, cleanses one of all sins.  Studying this life-affirming story of Rama ensures heavenly honor for a person, their family, and followers after death.  Furthermore, engaging with this narrative offers unique benefits to all social classes: Brahmins gain eloquence, Kshatriyas attain leadership, merchants prosper in trade, and Shudras achieve a higher status.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Canto LXIII

Brahma pays a visit to Viswamitra at Puskara and rewards him with
the status of a Rsi (seer). There he feels enamoured of Menaka,
a celestial nymph deputed by Indra, and enjoys life with
her for ten years. At last he realizes his folly and
retires to the Himalayas, where he renews his
austerities and is rewarded with the status of
a Maharsi by Brahma. Still dissatisfied,
he prosecutes his austerities with
renewed vigour
